experiments.title: How a personal ask to match a gift amount affects overall conversions in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personal ask to match a gift for potential donors will increase the conversion rate because it creates a sense of urgency and personal connection, motivating donors to feel that their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, especially when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
The American Cornerstone Institute sought to determine the impact of a more personal approach to soliciting a year-end gift match on overall conversion rates, including click-through rates from the email to the donation page and donation conversion rates.
They tested this concept through three emails sent during their year-end campaign. This write-up presents the results of email 3, which served as the kickoff email.
The control email presented the matching gift by stating that their gift would be doubled, thanks to a year-end matching gift challenge of up to $100,000. The treatment email presented the matching gift by saying that a generous donor wants to personally match their gift, allowing them to have double the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that utilizing a more personal approach when asking for a matching gift can significantly increase conversion rates. In this case, the treatment email, which presented the matching gift as coming from a generous donor wanting to match their gift personally, resulted in a 125.8% increase in donations compared to the control email.
This suggests that creating a sense of urgency and personal connection in donation appeals can motivate potential donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, particularly when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.
Regarding the effect on email click-through rate, the treatment email showed no difference in click-through rate. There was a 3.9% decrease in clicks with only a 42% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of personalization and emotional appeals in donation requests. By consistently incorporating these elements into fundraising campaigns, nonprofits like American Cornerstone Institute can further increase engagement, donations, and the overall success of their campaigns.

experiments.title: How a softer ask on a sticky bar impacts click rate and downstream donor metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the softer language on the sticky bar for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in clicks and downstream metrics because it leads to a larger pool of warm prospects who are more likely to donate once on the page to sign his card.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/26/2025
For Dr. Carson’s birthday this year, we tested the language on their website sticky bars to see which message resonated more with the audience: a direct ask like “Make my gift” or a softer invitation like “Sign the card.”
The test ran for about three weeks, with traffic split evenly between the two versions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using softer language on the sticky bar for homepage visitors, such as "Sign the card", resulted in a significant increase in clicks compared to a direct ask like "Make my gift". The treatment version saw a 425.7% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the softer approach resonated better with the audience.
More importantly, though, we wanted to see the downstream metrics on this test to measure the true impact of it.
Control:
21 gifts, $1,594 $75 Average gift
Treatment:
31 gifts, $876$28 Average gift
These results indicated a 46.7% lift in transactions (82.8% LOC), which is encouraging to see, but they also indicate a substantial drop in average gift.
In future experimentation, we should continue to test the use of softer language and invitations in our calls to action. By creating a more inviting and inclusive message, we can attract a larger pool of warm prospects and potentially increase the likelihood of donations. It is important to remember that the tone and language used in our communications can have a significant impact on audience engagement and conversion rates. By leveraging these learnings, we can optimize our messaging to better connect with our audience and drive meaningful actions.

experiments.title: How adding PayPal as a giving option on a funnel donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of PayPal for Pro-Life Donation Page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics  because  it provides a smooth and effortless giving experience, eliminating any extra hassle while also offering an additional way to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/20/2025 - 05/28/2025
For ACI’s Pro-Life Pledge donation page, we tested the integration of PayPal, leveraging Anedot’s native PayPal feature. Our goal was to assess whether adding PayPal would introduce any friction into the donation process before rolling it out to all users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that adding PayPal as a payment option to the Pro-Life Donation Page resulted in a notable 28% increase in transactions across all traffic. This outcome was achieved with a high level of statistical confidence at 96.5%, providing a solid foundation for future decision-making.
While we saw a general uptick in donations, we wanted to determine whether the increase was due to the availability of more payment options or if PayPal itself was driving the change.
Upon reviewing the data, we found that 30% of the donations on the test page were processed through PayPal. This suggests that PayPal is a trusted and credible giving method for ACI’s audience. Additionally, the donation lift closely aligns with the percentage of PayPal transactions, further supporting the impact of this payment option.
We plan to test this strategy in another ACI funnel to verify the results, and based on these findings, we’ll decide whether to implement PayPal across all ACI donation pages.

experiments.title: How adding Express Checkout options to a funnel donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of Express Checkout options (Google Pay & Apple Pay) for pledge donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it provides a smooth and effortless giving experience, eliminating any extra hassle while also offering additional ways to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/28/2025 - 06/13/2025
We wanted to test the addition of Express Checkout options, leveraging native Express Checkout features. Our goal was to assess whether these payment options (Google Pay & Apple Pay) would introduce any friction into the donation process before rolling it out to all users.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment suggest that the addition of Express Checkout options (Google Pay & Apple Pay) for the pledge donation page did not have the intended positive impact on donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment group experienced a significant decrease in transactions at 36.6% compared to the control group, with a statistical level of confidence of 99.9%.

Specifically, the one-time donation option saw a substantial 384% decrease in transactions within the treatment group. This indicates that offering Express Checkout options may have introduced friction into the donation process for visitors, leading to a lower conversion rate.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to carefully evaluate the impact of introducing new payment options on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to conduct smaller-scale tests or surveys before rolling out changes to a larger audience. Additionally, analyzing donor preferences and behavior patterns can help in making informed decisions about optimizing the donation process.

Overall, the results of this experiment highlight the importance of understanding the needs and preferences of donors when implementing changes to donation processes.

experiments.title: How a reputable quote impacts email conversion rate on a petition funnel
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of Dr. Carson’s quote for Pro-Life Pledge landing page visiors will achieve an increase in email conversions because the quote establishes trust and credibility for the visitor leading to higher engagement on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/18/2025
In an effort to boost the email conversion rate on ACI’s Pro-Life Petition landing page, we opted to test into adding a quote of Dr. Carson’s to the copy.
The quote read: “”I am committed to protecting innocent lives, especially when they are most vulnerable. The sanctity of life is something that we must wholeheartedly promote and enforce.” -Dr. Ben Carson”
Our hypothesis was that this addition would immediately establish trust and credibility for the visitor as Dr. Carson is a highly respected individual especially in the pro-life space.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the addition of Dr. Ben Carson's quote to the Pro-Life Petition landing page did not lead to an increase in email conversions as hypothesized. In fact, the treatment group showed a 5.0% decrease in Emails Acquired for All Traffic compared to the control group, with a statistical significance of 98.9%. This suggests that the quote did not have the intended effect of establishing trust and credibility for visitors, leading to higher engagement on the page.

While these results did not validate in the direction that we anticipated, they do offer a learning into the motivations behind ACI's audience.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the impact of any changes made to the landing page. While leveraging quotes from respected individuals can be a powerful tool, it is crucial to ensure that the messaging resonates with the target audience and aligns with the overall goals of the campaign. Additionally, it is important to continually test and iterate on different elements of the landing page to optimize conversion rates. This experiment highlights the importance of relying on data-driven insights to inform decision-making and the need to be open to adjusting strategies based on the results observed.

experiments.title: How a sliding bar on a campaign donation page allowing donors to adjust their impact influences conversion rate and revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that allowing donors to select the level of their impact dynamically will increase revenue during a high-urgency campaign by increasing the perceived clarity and appeal of the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2024
During ACI’s Giving Tuesday campaign, we wanted to increase the tangibility and clarity of the donor’s impact on the campaign’s donation page. The page already highlighted the number of people that would be reached with a donor’s gift. However, we hypothesized that allowing the donors to drag a slider that dynamically changes the number of people reached would increase the clarity of the offer when compared with the current  experience.
We used VWO to create a graphical, interactive slider on the page that dynamically changed the gift amount and the corresponding number of people the gift would reach. We split traffic 50/50 and all campaign sources were directed to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that introducing a graphical, interactive slider on the donation page, allowing donors to dynamically select the level of their impact, can significantly increase revenue during a high-urgency campaign. The treatment group saw a 28.9% increase in revenue for all traffic with a 95.7% level of confidence.
Although we saw a valid increase in revenue, we actually saw a 19.5% decrease in transactions with an 86.6% level of confidence.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore interactive elements that provide donors with a clear and tangible understanding of the impact of their donations. This includes incorporating dynamic features that allow donors to see in real-time how their contributions will make a difference. Additionally, we should aim to reach the minimum sample size required for statistical significance to ensure the reliability of the results.
Overall, it is crucial to prioritize clarity, personalization, and interactivity in donation pages to engage donors effectively and drive increased contributions, especially during high-urgency campaigns like Giving Tuesday.

experiments.title: How a more positive tone in copy impacts donor conversion metrics on a high urgency campaign donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the 2023 copy will perform better than the 2024 copy for Dr. Carson’s birthday card signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because we tend to use a more negative approach of the “what ifs” if you don’t make a gift which typically performs well for ACI.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2024 - 08/29/2024
Every year, ACI runs a high urgency campaign for Dr. Carson’s birthday for people to sign his card.
This year, we wanted to test running this year’s donation page copy vs last year’s copy. The 2024 copy had more visual styling on the page in the form of headers and more easy to consume paragraphs and sections as well as a heavier focus on Dr. Carson’s values and a positive tone.
The 2023 copy had a more negative and fearful approach to acquiring more donations and used a much more simple page style.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the 2023 copy, with its negative and fearful approach, did not perform better than the 2024 copy, which had a more positive tone and focused on Dr. Carson's values. In fact, the 2024 copy outperformed the 2023 copy significantly, with a 60.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic in just 3 days, with a statistical level of confidence of 96.0%.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the tone and messaging used in donation pages. While a negative approach may have worked well in the past for ACI, it is essential to continuously test and iterate on different messaging strategies to see what resonates best with donors. Additionally, it is important to continue utilizing A/B testing to gather data and insights on donor behaviors and preferences, which can inform future decision-making and strategy.

Overall, the results of this experiment emphasize the importance of testing different approaches and being open to new ideas and strategies to effectively engage donors and drive conversions.

experiments.title: How a one-step donation page vs a two-step donation page impacts donor conversion metrics during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a one-step funnel for Dr. Carson’s Birthday Card Signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the visitor would be forced to make a gift in order to submit their note.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/05/2024 - 09/10/2024
Every year, ACI runs a high urgency campaign focused around Dr. Ben Carson’s birthday in effort to encourage individuals to sign his birthday card and then make a gift.
This year, we decided to test a one-step funnel vs a two step funnel. The two-step funnel followed our normal funnel process: the visitor lands on a landing page asking them to sign Dr. Carson’s Birthday card. After signing it (filling out the form) they are directed to a donation page to make a gift.
The one-step funnel however combined the landing and donation page together. So, in this case, the visitor lands immediately on the donation page where there is an embedded form to sign Dr. Carson’s Birthday Card. The only way the visitors birthday wishes would be submitted was by completing a gift.
Our hypothesis was that the one-step funnel would increase our donor conversion metrics because the perception that they would not be able to send their birthday note to Dr. Carson would encourage them to make a gift.
This test ran as a Split URL test, split equally between all traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a one-step funnel for Dr. Ben Carson's Birthday Card Signers led to a significant increase in donor conversion metrics. The results showed a 30.9% increase in Transactions for All Traffic with a 95.8% confidence level, indicating that the treatment was successful in driving more donations.

Moreover, the One-Time Donation metric experienced a remarkable 352% increase with a 97% confidence level, further emphasizing the effectiveness of the one-step funnel approach in encouraging individuals to make a gift in order to submit their birthday note to Dr. Carson.

We were curious how the one-step funnel would impact revenue, and surprisingly, we sae a 54.6% lift in revenue at an 86.7% LOC. While invalid, these results suggest that individuals are more likely to give larger gifts in a one-step funnel.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of simplifying the donation process and creating a sense of urgency or exclusivity. By leveraging similar strategies, such as combining actions (e.g., signing a birthday card and making a donation) into a single step, nonprofits like the American Cornerstone Institute can potentially boost donor conversion rates and maximize fundraising efforts. Additionally, it is crucial to continue analyzing and testing different funnel approaches to identify the most effective methods for engaging supporters and driving donations.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the small percentage of users who donate impacted donor conversion on a petition donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out the low donor conversion rate for petition signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because   creating a sense of urgency and exclusivity to highlight a lack of follow-through and consistency will motivate more individuals to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2023 - 12/18/2023
ACI is a conservative organization that is guided by the four cornerstones: life, liberty, faith and community. They were running a petition to speak out against anti-life ideology and were driving a lot of traffic toward this offer. Once users signed the petition, they would be brought to a donation page asking them to make a donation to support the pro-life cause.
In this experiment, we wanted to test whether using expanded value proposition language would increase donor conversion on the donation page. The control page uses nonspecific language while the treatment page uses new language that highlights how “Less than 4% of people who [signed the petition] were willing to take the next step and [make a donation].”
Our hypothesis is that the additional language will increase donor conversion on this landing page by giving readers more clarity into WHY they should take the next step and support ACI’s work.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using expanded value proposition language around how little petition signers actually donate to support a cause they believe in can significantly increase donor conversion on a landing page. The treatment page, which highlighted the low conversion rate of petition signers, resulted in a 35.2% increase in Transactions and a 360% increase in One-Time Donations.
This suggests that creating a sense of urgency and exclusivity, along with highlighting a lack of follow-through and consistency, can motivate more individuals to donate. The language used in the treatment page resonated with users and led to a higher conversion rate.
Based on these findings, in future experimentation, it is recommended to continue using expanded value proposition language and focus on creating a sense of urgency and exclusivity. This can be achieved by emphasizing the impact and importance of the cause, as well as highlighting the need for support. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variations of language to further optimize conversion rates.
Overall, this experiment demonstrates the power of well-crafted value proposition language in driving conversions for nonprofit fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a continuation headline for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in the donor conversion rate because the continuation headline will provide more clarity on next steps for the visitors. because the continuation headline will provide more clarity on next steps for the visitors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/03/2023 - 11/20/2023
In an effort to optimize the instant donor conversion rate on our Pro-Life Statement Donation Page, we evaluated treatments to enhance user engagement. As a result, we implemented a continuation headline: “[First Name], your name has been added. Before you go, would you consider making a gift to fight for the unborn?”
We believe the original headline was inadvertently being perceived as a mere “thank you” page, potentially leading visitors to prematurely exit. Recognizing the need to convey a compelling message that extends beyond gratitude, the revised headline is designed to convey a sense of purpose and entice visitors to explore further down the page.
By using the new headline, visitors will know that there is more waiting for them on the page. Therefore, increasing engagement on the donation page resulting in a higher number of transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. The addition of a continuation headline significantly increased user engagement and donor conversion rate. The treatment saw a 31.4% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, with a high level of statistical confidence (97.2%).

2. The revised headline, which conveyed a sense of purpose and enticed visitors to explore further down the page, was more effective than the original headline that seemed like a mere "thank you" page.

3. Revenue experienced an 1.1% increase, while One-Time Donations saw a 33.4% increase, both with a high level of statistical confidence (97%). This indicates that the treatment not only increased donor conversion but also resulted in slightly higher donation amounts.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of messaging and headlines on user engagement and conversion rates. This experiment showed that a clear and compelling continuation headline can significantly improve user engagement and lead to higher conversion rates. It would be beneficial to test continuation copy throughout the body of the donation page to push even more visitors to the donation form to increase our instant donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar affects donor conversion rates on a CYE donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a humanized sticky bar on a CYE donation page for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it provides more urgency and a special message from a well-known figure at the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2023 - 12/31/2023
ACI ran their annual calendar year-end campaign. In the final four emails of the campaign, they wanted to test how an urgent, personalized sticky bar signed by Dr. Ben Carson would perform. Typically, they see higher conversion through funnels and campaigns when messaging is directly from Dr. Carson, so we expected an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a personalized sticky bar signed by Dr. Ben Carson did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version of the email had a 19.3% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control version, with a 95.1% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment version experienced a significant 237% decrease in One-Time Donations with a 98% level of confidence.

These results suggest that the personalized sticky bar did not create the urgency and special message that was expected. Instead, it may have had a negative impact on donor conversion.

To use this learning in future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and language used in fundraising campaigns. While using a well-known figure like Dr. Ben Carson may normally result in higher conversion rates, it is crucial to ensure that the messaging aligns with the organization's goals and resonates with potential donors. Additionally, it is important to conduct thorough testing and analysis before implementing new strategies to minimize potential negative impacts on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How using organization credibility language on a survey page affects email sign-up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe including background information about ACI and Dr. Ben Carson on the landing page will increase the percentage of survey takers because it will provide a sense of credibility and authority for visitors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/01/2023
ACI is a conservative organization that is guided by the four cornerstones: life, liberty, faith and community. They were running a survey to address the state of religious liberty in America and were driving paid Facebook traffic toward this offer. Once the user completes the survey, they will be brought to a donation page asking them to donate to support the organization and their efforts.
In this experiment, we wanted to test how adding more language around who Dr. Ben Carson is and what ACI is doing to protect individual liberties would affect the survey completion rate. The additional language gives more context about how the survey relates to the goals of ACI and provides more context about Dr. Ben Carson’s beliefs. Rather than asking the user directly if they are concerned about religious liberty in this country, we added language that allows the user to align their beliefs with both ACI and Dr. Ben Carson.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding background information about ACI and Dr. Ben Carson on the landing page did not increase the percentage of survey takers. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease of 10.9% in email acquisition with a 99.9% level of confidence compared to the control group.
This suggests that providing additional information about ACI and Dr. Ben Carson may not necessarily enhance credibility and authority for visitors. It is possible that the additional information may have overwhelmed or distracted the visitors, leading to a lower completion rate.
In future experimentation, it may be beneficial to focus on streamlining the landing page and making the survey process more straightforward and engaging. Rather than relying on extensive background information, it may be more effective to highlight the value and relevance of the survey to the visitors, and clearly communicate the purpose and potential impact of their participation.
Additionally, it would be valuable to analyze the qualitative feedback from the survey takers to identify any specific pain points or areas of improvement in the survey experience. This can help refine the survey process and increase completion rates in future experiments.

experiments.title: How suggesting a gift amount on an instant donation page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a $25 donation in the copy letter from Dr. Ben Carson and by pre-selecting it on the gift array will result in an increase in average gift and revenue overall because we’re removing the decision friction for the donor of what amount to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/06/2023 - 12/18/2023
ACI was running a petition to stand up against cancel culture. On the instant donation page following the petition page, users were asked to make a donation in support of ACI and their fight to find solutions against cancel culture. The control page had $25 pre-selected in the gift array, but there was no mention of this ask in the copy from Dr. Ben Carson.
Because the average gift on this page is $19, we thought asking the user to make a $25 gift in the copy would remove decision friction and increase overall average gift and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that asking for a specific donation amount in the copy letter and pre-selecting it on the gift array led to a 68% decrease in conversion rate with a 99% level of confidence. There was no difference in revenue or average gift.
The control version, on the other hand, did not mention a specific amount in the copy and had a $25 pre-selected option in the gift array. This version performed better in terms of donation generation.
Based on these results, it is clear that the specific ask of a $25 donation created decision friction for the donors. By removing this ask from the copy and pre-selecting a default option in the gift array, the control version was able to provide a more frictionless giving experience and generate higher revenue.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of specific donation asks and pre-selected options on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to test different donation amounts, language, and default options to optimize for higher revenue and average gift. Additionally, it would be prudent to carefully analyze the potential consequences of any changes in copy or design to ensure that they align with the objectives of the nonprofit and do not negatively impact donation performance.

experiments.title: How using a pledge vs a direct account registration ask through Facebook Lead Ads affects sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe we’ll receive cheaper names through a pledge lead ad than through a direct account creation ask because signing a pledge is less of a commitment.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/05/2023
This organization is trying to acquire more new accounts for its school curriculum program. We wanted to see how lead ads would affect sign up rate and cost per new account, but we decided to test two different types of lead ads to see which “ask” performed better.
The first type was a teacher pledge. This Facebook ad led to a form in Facebook where they submit their name and email address to sign the pledge. Then their account is created using a zapier connection. Their log in information is emailed to them.
The second type was a direct ask to make an account. This ad lef to a form in Facebook where they submitted their name and email address to create their account. It was created and their log in information was emailed to them.
Although the first type is an indirect ask to create an account, we anticipated we’d receive cheaper names since the ask is more straight-forward, and has less commitment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the teacher pledge ask performed better than the direct account creation ask in terms of acquiring emails. The treatment saw a 25.7% decrease in emails acquired compared to the control. This finding is statistically significant with a 100.0% level of confidence.

Based on this result, it is recommended to continue using the teacher pledge ask in future experimentation for acquiring new accounts. This type of ask leads to more email acquisitions and is more effective in generating new accounts.

experiments.title: How calling out a most popular gift option on a gift array affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the inclusion of a “most popular” callout on a gift array  for the $50 option for potential donors will achieve a higher average gift because it leverages the psychological principle of social proof, creating a sense of trust and legitimacy among potential donors.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/23/2023
The American Cornerstone Institute is offering a book called “Why America Matters” for a gift of $25 or more through paid Facebook ads. We wanted to test adding a most popular callout to the $50 option on the gift array to see if donors would give a larger amount to receive the book.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a "most popular" callout on a gift array for a specific option can significantly increase donations and average gift. The treatment version, which included a "most popular" callout on the $50 option, resulted in a 27.5% increase in donations compared to the control version. Additionally, the treatment saw a $0.35 increase in gift size with a 95.46% level of confidence.
This experiment demonstrates the power of social proof, where potential donors are influenced by the choices made by others. By highlighting a specific option as the "most popular," potential donors are more likely to trust and perceive it as a legitimate choice, leading to a higher average gift.
In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the use of social proof in different ways. For example, we can test different callouts such as "best value" or "preferred choice" to see if they have a similar impact on donor behavior. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore the effect of social proof in other aspects of the donation process, such as the donation page layout or testimonials from previous donors.

experiments.title: How clarity affects clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the clarity of the ask for Facebook visitors will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because they understand what we are asking them to do..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/08/2023
Ben Carson’s American Cornerstone Institute was running ads promoting their Little Patriots Curriculum. On review, the ads that had performed best seemed to have a mismatch of creative and text. “Liberty the Eagle” was featured in the image, while the text asked the reader to pledge to teach kids founding principles.
Liberty the Eagle is a character in the Little Patriots curriculum, but that’s only encountered after they sign up — which means at the point of seeing the ad, the reader has no idea who Liberty is. They decided to replace this with a picture of Dr. Carson, a well-known figure. Once they did that, they realized they could change the “sign the pledge” ask to a “statement of belief” ask that is followed by the “sign the pledge” ask.
They launched two copy variants of the new ads to their audiences to see what worked best.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key learnings from this experiment include:
1. The importance of aligning the creative and text in an ad: The mismatch between the image of "Liberty the Eagle" and the text asking for a pledge to teach kids founding principles resulted in lower clickthrough rates. This suggests that the audience may not have understood the ask or the connection between the image and text.
2. Clarity in the call-to-action: Increasing the clarity of the ask for Facebook visitors led to a significant increase in clicks. This emphasizes the importance of clearly communicating the desired action or response from the audience.
3. The significance of statistical confidence: The treatment group showed a 218.6% increase in donations with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This high level of statistical confidence suggests that the results are reliable and not due to random chance.
To utilize these learnings in future experimentation, it is crucial to ensure that the creative and text in ads are aligned and coherent. Additionally, clear and explicit calls-to-action should be employed to improve engagement and conversions. It is also important to continue using statistical analysis to determine the significance of the results and validate the effectiveness of the changes made.

We were running a monthly upgrade ask to existing donors on the housefile and wanted to offer a special premium for those that chose to convert. In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the inclusion of images or visual elements on donation pages. It is also important to consider other factors that may affect donor conversion, such as the placement and design of the image, the clarity of the premium offer, and any potential friction caused by the addition of the image.
It is recommended to conduct additional A/B tests with larger sample sizes and optimize the design and placement of the image to ensure that the results are valid and reliable. experiments.title: How using “Learn More” as a CTA impacted FB clicks on a direct ask
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reducing the level of commitment in the CTA language for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clicks and donation page views because the subtle ask to “learn more” introduces less friction than the direct ask to “donate now”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/29/2023
We were running a direct ask campaign on Facebook using the “Donate Now” button as the CTA and wondered if using a less direct CTA would help drive more donation page visitors. We kept all other elements of the ad the same and changed the button copy to “Learn More”. Our hypothesis was the “easier” option would create less friction by reducing the anxiety in the minds of the visitors of potentially making the full commitment without having enough information to make a decision first. If we could get them to give a “micro-yes” to learning more about the program we were asking them to support, we believed we could bring more visitors to the donation page from the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that changing the CTA from "Donate Now" to "Learn More" resulted in a significant increase in clicks and donation page views. The treatment version, which had the "Learn More" CTA, saw a 48.8% increase in clicks for all traffic compared to the control version with 97.7% level of confidence so the experiment results are valid. We also saw a 30% decrease in cost per click on the "Learn More" version.
This suggests that the direct ask of "Donate Now" may have created friction and anxiety for the viewers, causing them to hesitate or be reluctant to click on the ad. By offering a lower level of commitment with the "Learn More" CTA, viewers may have felt more comfortable and willing to engage with the ad.
When reviewing results, we also noted that one additional reason the treatment could have outperformed the control is that Facebook, by default, includes a subheader message when the "Donate Now" button is selected that says "Not affiliated with Meta" which could also be introducing more friction for visitors and introducing anxiety if the message appears suspicious or untrustworthy. This default subheader also replaces the available room to display the full headline so the treatment version has the benefit of showing the full headline of "Help equip future conservative leaders" which offers more clarity than the cut off version "Help equip future..."

experiments.title: How a multi-step donation process impacted donations for an acquisition offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a multi-step donation process broken down into three short steps instead of one long step for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because donors will appreciate the easy flow of the multi-step donation process and will encounter less friction as they complete their donation one simple step at a time.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 06/09/2023
ACI’s donation platform now has the functionality to set up a multiple-step donation process. In this instance, the standard one-step process would be divided into three steps, one to select the donation amount, one to provide contact/billing information, and one to add credit card information. We want to test the one-step vs. the three-step donation process for a popular content offer to see how donors respond. Would three shorter steps lead to more donation conversions than one long step?
We have set up this experiment for their Cancel Culture Pledge, and traffic will be split 50/50 between the one-step and multi-step process for the duration of the experiment. Let’s see how donors respond.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Donors did not respond to the multi-step donation process as we hypothesized. In fact, the treatment validated with a 51.9% decrease in donations after running for only 14 days. The treatment also produced a valid 72.3% decrease in revenue.
Possible explanations for the negative results could be that most donors are accustomed to the standard one-step donation process and this multi-step process led to increased anxiety and possibly confusion. There is also the possibility that the three-step process invited more friction instead of less, as we hypothesized. Instead of having all the necessary information directly in front of them, donors encountered an uncertain number of steps and also more opportunities to flee the donation opportunity. Perhaps adding clarity to the three-step process would improve the donor experience and save conversions. This could be communicated by noting that this is step 1 of 2 or 2 of 3 as they go through the process.
Some tweaks are clearly needed if we want to try testing this multi-step process once more with our audience.

experiments.title: How clarity in a subject line impacted email clicks

rows.name: Looking out for your child’s education
A free educational program for kids

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity to a subject line for email recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks because recipients will gain a strong initial idea of what the message is about and what it might offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/25/2023 - 06/08/2023
We want to test three different subject lines for ACI’s Little Patriots Pledge email to see which generates the most clicks. The purpose of the email is to send recipients to the pledge landing page where they would pledge to use and expand the Little Patriots curriculum.
The control subject line broadly mentions education
Treatment 1 subject line mentions a specific type of education
Treatment 2 subject line mentions an actual product – a “free educational program”
Let’s see which approach towards clarity in a subject line generates the most email clicks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Clarity proved to be the key to this subject line test. Each treatment produced a valid increase in email clicks. Treatment 1 produced a 145.8% increase in clicks while treatment 2 produced a 158.7% increase.

Our audience responded well to clarity. They appreciated knowing more information as they were considering opening the email, and surely the "free" callout in treatment two resonated strongly with them.

Interestingly, when looking at open rate, treatment 2 resulted in a decrease while treatment 1 resulted in a 2.6% increase with a 98% level of confidence. So treatment 2 produced a significant increase in opens and clicks.

Moving forward, we should implement more subject lines that focus on clarity, giving our audience up front transparency and value.

experiments.title: How adding privacy language below a survey sign up form impacted sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a more formal privacy statement at the bottom of the form will increase the education survey sign up rate because it will establish more credibility and trust in ACI and the survey itself.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/28/2023
ACI’s Education Survey is producing a 43.3% sign up rate over the last month. While this metric is performing above-benchmark, we believe there is room for improvement for this offer to match the sign up rates of other offers such as the Cancel Culture Pledge (59.5% sign up rate).
In reviewing the current landing page, we wonder if emphasizing the privacy language below the form will build trust with our audience and lead a higher percentage of our audience to submit their survey. We’ve seen this tactic work across other similar organizations.
People can feel vulnerable giving away their information, so adding this language could provide them more comfort and trust and it may be the added punch that this page needs to improve sign up rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a more formal privacy statement at the bottom of the form did not increase the Education Survey sign up rate for ACI. The treatment saw a 4.3% decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic with a 99.5% statistical level of confidence, indicating that the control version performed better.
An important metric to review regarding data privacy is new visitors, as they may not have strong familiarity with the organization they'd be giving their information to. In this case, new visitors produced a valid 4.7% decrease in sign up rate, showing that they were actually less likely to submit their information when viewing the data privacy language.
Interestingly, donation revenue increased by 23.7% with an 84.1% level of confidence. Although it did not validate, the results are encouraging and show that those who did build trust through the landing page privacy language did give more once they reached the donation page.
The downstream metrics tell us that we're onto something with this approach. So as a next test, we will test shorter, more direct privacy language and place it below the submit button as a way to maintain the user momentum. We hypothesize that because the privacy language was placed between the form and the submit button for this experiment, this may have introduced friction, making users think twice about  submitting their information. The language should compliment the sign up process instead of impede it.

experiments.title: How adding a continuation headline and personalizing the body copy to come from a prominent figure impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a continuation headline matched with personalizing the donation value prop copy for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because more visitors will engage with the value prop and will be more motivated to give due to the personal message coming from the organization’s founder.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/21/2023
ACI’s Cancel Culture Pledge funnel is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. The entry rate and sign up rate are above benchmark for the offer, which presents an opportunity to optimize the offer at the donation page level to see if we can improve our donor conversion rate.
Evaluating the current donation page, what first stood out is the headline. By simply thanking someone for signing up for the pledge, users may assume that they’ve reached the end of the engagement. Most consumer transactions conclude with a thank you, but we want visitors to stick around to engage with the value proposition copy and make a donation.
That’s why we developed a treatment variant (treatment #1) with the following headline: “Your name has been added. Before you go, would you consider this special request from Dr. Ben Carson?”
Through this headline, visitors will know that something important is waiting for them, and they should therefore continue on in their engagement, which is exactly what we want for them to do.
We then transitioned to the body copy and wondered if the value proposition message would resonate more strongly with potential donors if it was presented in the first person. We felt particularly confident in this assessment given the notoriety of ACI’s Founder.
Thus, we developed treatment #2, reframing the body copy to be presented from the 1st person point of view. We kept the copy consistent with it’s previous version, only adjusting the point of view. In addition, we added the signature of the messenger to add an even more personal touch.
Let’s see how each treatment performed.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a continuation headline and personalizing the donation value proposition copy for potential donors can significantly increase donations.
This combination resulted in a 114% increase in donor conversion rate with a valid 96.6% level of confidence. It outperformed the "headline only" treatment, which resulted in a 95.4% increase in donations with a nearly valid 93.2% level of confidence.
Treatment #2 was especially effective among new visitors, which produced a valid 322.6% increase in donations. This treatment also produce a valid lift in revenue.
The continuation headline led more visitors to the value proposition copy, and the personalized message within created a stronger emotional connection with potential donors, motivating them to engage with the value proposition and ultimately donate.
In future experimentation, we should continue to test the impact of personalized messaging and content on donor conversion rates. We can also experiment with different ideas for continuation headlines to find the most effective approach for engaging visitors and encouraging them to donate. Additionally, we can test the impact of personalization on other key metrics, such as user engagement and retention.

experiments.title: How using an organic-looking post for a Facebook ad affects click-through rate and other downstream metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making an ad look like an organic post for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher click-through rate because it feels more human and not like advertising.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2023 - 05/02/2023
Several organizations have tested using an organic-looking post as a Facebook ad and have seen a significant decrease in cost per click and a significant increase in click-through rate compared to standard marketing ads.
ACI is testing this concept on its Pro-Life petition campaign in Facebook. To test this, we built two “organic” ads that consisted of just an image to make it appear as an organic post. We will compare the performance of these two ads to our original top-performing ad, which is a standard marketing ad with ad copy and an image.
The ads are sharing the same budget and are targeting the same audiences in Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our organic posts did not provide the results we were looking for. Treatment 1 saw a 56.6% increase in click-through rate with a 100% level of confidence and treatment 2 saw a 10.8% increase also with a 62.8% level of confidence. While click through rate is improved, other initial metrics including CPM and CPC worsened, and end-of-funnel metrics (donations and GCPD) also worsened compared to the control.
Spend - Control ($1,445), Treatment 1 ($297), Treatment 2 (70)
CPM - Control ($3.60), Treatment 1 ($9.20), Treatment 2 ($5.45)
CPC - Control ($0.59), Treatment 1 ($1.01), Treatment 2 ($0.80)
% Sign Up - Control (34.2%), Treatment 1 (61.5%) - 35.6% increase in email conversion with a 100 LoC, Treatment 2 (53.5%)
ICR - Control (6.51%), Treatment 1 (3.16%) - 51.4% decrease with a 95.4% LoC, Treatment 2 (0.00%)
GCPN - Control ($2.09, Treatment 1 ($1.88), Treatment 2 (1.83)
GCPD - Control ($-2), Treatment ($32), Treatment 2 (null)
ROI - Control (1.06), Treatment 1 (0.45), Treatment 2 (null)
Based on the results of other organizations, this type of treatment typically produces higher CTR, lower CPC and CMP, and therefore lower GCPD. In this instance, the treatments resulted in higher CTR, higher CPC, and higher GCPD. CPMs for the control were also better than the two treatments.
The overall learning is that organic-looking ads did not perform at the beginning of the funnel - higher CPC and higher CPMs - nor at the end of the funnel - lower ICR and higher GCPD.
Image and likeness of ACI's Founder Dr. Ben Carson historically leads to high performance. The control ad for this experiment includes an image of Dr. Carson, which may contribute significant engagement at the beginning and throughout the funnel.

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making an ad look like an organic post for Facebook viewers will achieve a higher click-through rate because it feels more human and not like advertising.

Several organizations have tested using an organic-looking post as a Facebook ad and have seen a significant decrease in cost per click and a significant increase in click-through rate compared to standard marketing ads.
ACI is testing this concept on its Pro-Life petition campaign in Facebook. To test this, we built two “organic” ads that consisted of just an image to make it appear as an organic post. experiments.keyLearnings:
Our organic posts did not provide the results we were looking for. Treatment 1 saw a 23.3% decrease in click-through rate with a 99.9% level of confidence and treatment 2 saw a 44.1% decrease also with a 99.9% level of confidence. Initial metrics and downstream metrics proved discouraging with the exception of petition sign up rate:

Based on the results of other organizations, this type of treatment typically produces higher CTR, lower CPC and CMP, and therefore lower GCPD. However, in this instance, the treatments resulted in lower CTR, higher CPC, and higher GCPD. CPMs did decrease with the treatments, though only marginally for treatment 1.
Notably, the sample size was out of whack. Next time we run this type of experiment, we should split the treatment(s) into a separate campaign and split the overall campaign budget between the control and treatment to ensure relatively equal spend. Still, the sample sizes were significant enough here to exceed the required sample size and reach a significant level of confidence.
The overall learning is that organic-looking ads did not perform at the beginning of the funnel - higher CPC than the control and lower CTR - nor at the end of the funnel - lower ICR and higher GCPD.

experiments.title: How adding testimonials to an education program sign up page impacted sign ups
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including parent testimonials of the program on the main registration page will result in an increase in email acquisition because the social proof of other’s reviews will encourage more people to sign up for the program.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/14/2023 - 04/14/2023
For this experiment, we wanted to test how adding testimonials from parents about the value of the Little Patriots program would affect the email acquisition rate for ACI.
We split traffic 50/50 – 50% received the control registration page while the other 50% received the treatment version which included the testimonials/reviews.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including parent testimonials on the registration page does not necessarily lead to an increase in email acquisition rates. In fact, the treatment version with testimonials saw a 10.4% decrease in email acquisitions for all traffic, with a 96.1% level of confidence that this result was statistically significant. This suggests that the testimonials may have been distracting to the overall goal of email acquisition, and added unnecessary friction to the registration process.
The treatment saw a 9.2% decrease in email acquisition among new visitors with a 90% level of confidence. This is encouraging as even new visitors to Little Patriots come to the registration page already confident in the product. They do not need testimonials to convert.
Based on these findings, future experimentation should focus on testing the impact of testimonials on different types of visitors, as well as exploring alternative forms of social proof, such as trust badges or customer ratings. Additionally, it may be useful to test the impact of different types of language and messaging on the registration page to identify the most effective call-to-action for each type of visitor.

experiments.title: How images and video of a personality impacted clicks on an eBook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using images and videos of a well-known figure for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because this introduces more credibility and authority than using an eBook image alone.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/02/2023
When running ads for an eBook offer, we wondered if the cover of the eBook was appealing enough to drive interest from Facebook ad viewers. In previous testing on other offers, we had found success in using the organization’s founder’s name and photo. We wondered if we could achieve similar success by featuring images and videos in the ads that were relevant to the eBook’s contents. The first treatment had a background of an American flag to serve as a “trigger image” that can help with familiarity as users scroll. The second treatment was a 30-second video quoting Martin Luther King, Jr. and the third treatment was a 30-second video urging the American people to work together and not treat each other as enemies. We launched all versions of the ad and let them run for 30 days.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we saw valid decreases in clickthrough rate for the first and second treatments, the third treatment that included a 30-second video urging the American people to work together and not treat each other as enemies achieved a 44.0% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. We believe this video saw a lift in engagement and effectiveness due to the relevancy of the messaging that gave a sneak peek into how the topic of the eBook was discussed. The video that produced the lift also had an enhanced sense of urgency compared to the other treatments that could have positively impacted performance. Future experimentation should focus on incorporating relevant and credible images and videos in the ad campaigns, accompanied by concise and compelling messages that resonate with the target audience.

experiments.title: How a more direct call to action in a Facebook ad impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that concisely communicating the call to action for Facebook ad viewers will achieve an increase in clickthrough rate because it kicks off the cognitive momentum to keep things congruent with the landing page copy.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/02/2023
In a previous test on this offer, we tested short copy vs. longer copy and found that the longer copy version motivated more visitors to click the ad. In this next iteration, we wanted to test back into using shorter copy but installing the learning from the previous experiment which was that visitors needed clarity about the pledge they would be signing before they would commit to taking the next step. So this time, we kept the outline of the four pillars they would be agreeing to in the pledge in the copy, but removed all the extra lead-in copy. The treatment also introduced checkbox emojis to indicate each statement of the pledge they would be agreeing with if they signed.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that concise communication of the call to action in Facebook ads significantly increases clickthrough rates. By removing unnecessary lead-in copy and emphasizing the four pillars of the pledge with checkbox emojis, the treatment achieved a 15.2% increase in clicks for all traffic with a 100.0% statistical level of confidence. This suggests that visitors respond well to clear, concise messaging that ensures congruence with the landing page. In future experiments, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of concise messaging and strong calls to action that align with the landing page content.

experiments.title: How reducing friction in an instant donation form impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor response because reducing the form length makes it feel like less work for the visitor and improves cognitive momentum.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2023 - 02/25/2023
The donation page software we were using rolled out a new update which changed the donation page template to offer express checkout options and allowed returning donors to “sign in” to complete their donation. We were curious if these changes to the donation page template were introducing unnecessary friction into the process and since these changes were automatically rolled out across all donation pages, we wanted to verify if the new template was helping or hurting donation page conversion. We developed a treatment that hid the express checkout and login options and kept all other elements of the donation page and form identical. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for one week.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for one week, we observed an overall 41% increase in donations with a 99.7% statistical level of confidence so the results are valid. Our hypothesis was correct that the newly implemented donation page template was hindering donation page conversion by increasing friction on the form. Additionally, we saw a 366% increase in revenue on the treatment version.
The key takeaway from this experiment is that reducing friction, such as login and express checkout options, and making the donation process simpler helped increase cognitive momentum in the process and improved donor conversion. To further validate the results, we should continue to test this same concept on other donation page placements outside of instant donation pages, including the main donation page and high urgency campaigns where visitor motivation may differ.

experiments.researchQuestion:
We believe that
talking about Dr. Carson as the reason behind the crash rather than focusing on just the large volume of people for email subscribers will achieve an increase of clicks because people will be more motivated if moved to action by someone they trust over a large volume of unknown people.

experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2023 - 02/10/2023
The American Cornerstone Institute was launching their new Little Patriots site. Due to some unexpected press coverage, they got a flood of traffic to the new site which brought it down. They wanted to use this as an opportunity to update their subscribers on the impact and let them know that the site is now accessible. This email proved to be an opportunity to drive more traffic to the program so they decided to run a test on what content would drive more interest. They decided to test two angles: “Popularity” vs. “Dr. Carson” with popularity being about the huge interest nationwide and Dr. Carson being about how his activities are tied to these efforts. This email was sent to a small subset of the subscriber file with the winner being rolled out the the entire audience.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learning from this experiment is that talking about Dr. Carson as the reason for the crash of the site was not more motiviating than just talking about the huge interest throughout the country. This suggests that users are more likely to respond to tangible and proven success stories versus abstract stories, even if they involve a figure they trust. This can help inform future experiments and help target audiences more accurately with messaging that’s more likely to resonate. We should focus on stories and results that are easy to understand, have visual appeal, and showcase authoritative figures that evoke trust and have real-world results.

During calendar year-end, we sent an offer to the housefile asking them to commit to ACI’s Four Pillars Pledge. The email version included a preview of all four belief statements they would be asked to sign on the landing page, so we wanted to test including this in the Facebook ad as well. Not only did this improve our email conversion rate, but we also saw a 14% lift on donations downstream with 53% level of confidence. Additional benefits were that the treatment ad reduced cost per name by 21.5% and reduced net cost per donor by 44.8%. Going forward, we will run the long version of the ad as the new control and develop a new hypothesis to run against it to see if we can continue to improve performance.

experiments.title: How opening a loop of curiosity via the subject line affects open rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a less direct subject line will increase open rates by creating an open loop of curiosity that recipients will want to close by opening the emails to learn more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/14/2022
During a year-end campaign when we know potential donors will be receiving a high volume of emails soliciting donations, we wanted to know whether or not using less direct subject lines would improve open rates by opening a loop of curiosity that the recipient would want to close by opening the email.
Recipients of this campaign were segmented between previous donors and non-donors and roughly half of both segments were sent emails with subject lines that spoke directly to the contents of the email while the other half received emails that opened a loop of curiosity with less direct subject lines … but which (importantly) would still clearly relate to the subject matter of the email once the recipient opened. This test was run across three appeal emails in an effort to ensure the results were not isolated to a single send.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at the results of this experiment a number of interesting observations came to light:
The curiosity-building subject lines did indeed increase open rates by 2.6% (99.9% LoC)However, the curiosity-building subject lines also decreased click-through rates by -8.5% (97.4% LoC)
But because these sends were all segmented between previous donors and non-donors, drilling down into the data may help paint a more complete picture of our results, for instance:
Among non-donors:
Curiosity-building subject lines produced a 2.4% increase in open rates (100% LoC)But they also produced a 16.6% decrease in click-through rates  (100% LoC)
Among previous donors:
Curiosity-building subject lines produced a 4% increase in open rates (100% LoC)While also producing a 33.8% increase in click-through rates (99.9% LoC)
And so we see that not all recipients behave the same, further emphasizing the importance of tailoring communications to the audience that you are targeting, and doing away with the assumption that all audiences behave the same.
Non-donors may be less familiar with (or less bought into) the value American Cornerstone Institute provides—hence their status as non-donors—while previous donors, who better understand (or better align) with the value received from ACI were hooked by the curiosity-building subject line and continued their momentum through the appeal contained inside the email, ultimately clicking through to the donation page at a significantly higher rate.
Based on these results, we will want to send emails with direct subject lines to the non-donor segment while sending curiosity-building emails to the previous donor segments as the results from this experiment have clearly indicated each segment's preference.

experiments.title: How the placement of a call to action impacted email acquisitions on the homepage
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that communicating the value prop in a more prominent location for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in program sign ups.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/22/2022 - 12/28/2022
As we began driving paid traffic to the Little Patriots home page from multiple channels, we were noticing both high bounce rates (58%) and low traffic to the registration page (3.28%) which made us wonder if it was clear to new visitors how to sign-up for Little Patriots upon arriving to the website. In reviewing the site, there were multiple calls to action that seemed equally weighted and there was a lot of friction in the way interfering users from clearly understanding how and why to sign-up for the program. We discovered that the MOST important action we wanted users to take was being presented as the LEAST visible element on the page, likely causing visitors to miss it altogether. Since the goal for visitors was to sign up, we were curious if instead of having the sign up info presented at the bottom of the page, if we could increase conversion by putting the sign up info in the top content block on the page to reduce confusion about what the desired call to action was in order to drive higher name acquisition rates.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the experiment for a week, was that the treatment with the value proposition and call to action placed at the top of the page increased email acquisition rate by 154.9% with 99% level of confidence. We believe this is because we moved the content block promoting and communicating the value of the extremely high-value free coursework which serves as the main driver of name acquisition for LP from being buried at the very bottom of the page where visitors had to hunt for the information. We made it easier immediately upon arrival to understand what the desired action was by removing confusion and friction: were visitors supposed to watch a video? explore programs and new episodes? make a gift? These were all options on the page that had varying weights assigned by their placement and level of which they were featured on the site. When we gave visitors a clear entry point at the top of the page and communicated the WHY, we found that more visitors took the intended action to sign up. Going forward, we will roll out the treatment design in order to improve the visibility of Little Patriots coursework while reducing friction and improving UX in an effort to increase name acquisition rates to make better use of the paid advertising dollars being spent to drive traffic to the site.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to an instant donation page affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a humanized sticky bar featuring a well-known public figure on an instant donation page will increase donation rates by adding value and credibility to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2022 - 12/06/2022
For a content offer for a public policy client’s pledge, we wanted to know how adding a humanized sticky bar including the image of a well-known public figure on a donation page would affect donation rates.
This experiment ran as an A/B test with roughly 50% of traffic being shown the treatment including the sticky bar and roughly 50% of traffic shown the control, sans the sticky bar. No other elements were changed.
Because of the large volume of traffic the donation page received, we were able to validate the results of this experiment after only 2 days.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 48 hours, we observed a 38.6% INCREASE in donation rates, with a 96.9% level of confidence, among traffic who were shown the treatment including the humanized sticky bar, validating our hypothesis that a humanized sticky bar would increase donation rates.
We believe that the increase in donations occurred because the large and colorful sticky bar, featuring a well-known and well-respected public figure among this organization's prospective donors, increased the visibility of the perceived impact for potential donors.
The sticky bar also invites the user to "join" this public figure in achieving the outcome they want to achieve, while additional creating a message match from the pledge page seen before the donation page, as the pledge referred directly to the four values being presented in the sticky bar, creating momentum toward the conversion and leveraging the persuasion principle of "consistency" to inspire the user to follow up on their pledge by taking action.
It is also worth noting that the treatment including the sticky bar produced a ~15% higher average gift, perhaps because the additional value statement shown in the sticky bar increased the perceived value of a larger donation in the minds of donors.
Because of the positive results of this experiment, we will look for other opportunities to include a sticky bar on instant donation pages in future acquisition efforts.

experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/05/2022
In an effort to increase click thru rate in email prospecting for a public policy client, we wanted to measure the effect of adding credibility and interest to the offer by means of utilizing an image of a well known personality.
The offer deployed was a children’s book premium offer. The control version used the image of the children’s book, while the treatment version utilized the image of the well-known personality who wrote the book.
This test was set up in which ~50% of the audience would get the control version and ~50% of the audience would get the treatment version. However, once results were clear to email brokers they only deployed the winning version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 61.5% increase in click through rate (with a 99.9% level of confidence) on the treatment creative, we were pleased to conclude with clarifying results the validity of our hypothesis. By adding an image of a well-known personality, the image will serve to build credibility, intrigue, and interest with the audience.
Additionally, while not statistically significant, but still significant to note, the relative difference in donors with increased click thru rate was 76.2%.
The control version received 4 donations while the treatment variant received 28 donors—a 76.2% increase. While the level of confidence was only 79.5% and did not meet the 95% threshold to validate, we still believe these results are still of importance to note.
These results are a positive indication that similar tests should be run for other public policy and faith-based clients that can utilize well-known personalities for marketing purposes.

experiments.title: How introducing “donors like you” language impacted clicks in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that casting an email recipient into the role of a donor before they donate will increase click-through rates by using the persuasion principle of consistency to prompt them to follow through.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/12/2022 - 07/27/2022
In an effort to increase click-through rates, we wanted to know how “casting” the recipient into the role of donor before they donate would impact engagement. In his experiment, we kept all other copy the same, switching only language that mentions needing “your help” to “help from donors like you”. We sent each version of the email to 50% of the house file to assess the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By casting the email recipient into the role of a donor, we were able to realize a 17.8% lift in click-through rates, with a 97.5% level of confidence, validating our hypothesis. We believe this lift occurred because by asking for help from "donors like you" we preemptively created a feeling of commitment which prompted more people to seek consistency by clicking through.

While past experiments have shown that "you" language increase engagement, we wanted to take this a step further by speaking to a future you that is already making a difference by donating to the cause at hand.

This treatment achieves two things en route to increasing engagement:
It lets the recipient know that they are just the kind of person the client needs to help them accomplish their goal, and that those who do donate are people who align with similar values and goals
 	By casting the recipient into the role of a donor before they donate, the potential donor wants to be consistent by translating their values into action. The thought pattern "I care about these issues and I am someone to whom this organization is looking to help, but am unwilling to take actions" creates an uncomfortable feeling of cognitive dissonance wherein the potential donor feels one way but does not back that up with their actions. This treatment gives them the opportunity to put their values into action, thus increasing engagement

Would they be more motivated to open and take the intended desired action from an email with a subject line that did not include curiosity and rather gave clarity, revealing the contents of the message? Or would they be more compelled to open and click a message that related to an issue they care about and promises a reveal. experiments.keyLearnings: We measured comparative open rates for each version of the email and saw a 4.3% lift in opens on the treatment version. So we wanted to dig a little deeper to investigate the overall results on email engagement. We also measured the number of clicks on the Call to Action in the body copy for each version. Although the emails were identical aside from the subject line, we saw a 61.4% increase in click rate with 100% level of confidence on the treatment. We believe this is due to the positive momentum the subject line carries from "This is great news" into reminding people it wasn't too late to submit their survey. If the reader was curious to see what the great news was, they had an increased chance of seeing the call to action inviting them to complete the survey as well. And since the message leaned into aligning with the subscriber's values and highlighted social proof, they could now have a compelling reason to say "yes" to the ask and be a part of the continued great news.
