experiments.title: How adding language that conveys the value of joining a monthly newsletter near the form affects email acquisition rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that conveying the value of joining a monthly newsletter will increase email acquisition by giving visitors a tangible reason to sign up.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/31/2022 - 11/17/2022
In an effort to improve email acquisition rates of website visitors on the homepage, we wanted to know if conveying the value of joining, and what visitors can expect to receive when signing up, would increase the rate at which people were willing to sign up.
Previously there was very little supporting copy near the signup form, with only the phrase “Subscribe to receive updates and event invitations.” presented in small font beneath the form.
For this test, we split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for just over two weeks before validating our results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 17 days, we were able to validate a 71.2%  lift in email acquisitions from visitors who saw the treatment page.
For the treatment, we wanted to convey enough value, social proof, and exclusivity to give visitors an enticing reason to submit their email addresses and agree to receive email updates from this public policy organization.
To add social proof: we leveraged the considerable size of the organization's housefile (146,000+) to express to the visitor that many other people with the same concerns, values, and interests have already signed upTo add exclusivity: we made it clear that they would be receiving emails from Dr. Ben Carson, a well-known and admired public figure, when they signed up. We also used the verbal cue, "exclusive, subscriber-only content" to reinforce this exclusivityTo add value: we clarified what subscribers receive when they sign up ... updates, educational content that teaches America's founding principles and delivers expert explanations, key insights, and critical analysis on the toughest challenges facing our nation today.
This experiment validated our hypothesis that simply asking someone to give you their email address with a generic sign-up prompt is not enough to earn their trust, pique their interest, and motivate them to join. Instead, organizations should consider what value subscribers receive when they join (what's in it for them), and communicate exactly WHY someone should subscribe and WHAT sets your email updates apart from other organizations.
Email marketing remains one of the most cost-effective, powerful tools an organization has to grow their housefile, build advocacy, and convert them into generous donors ... but as people become more and more selective about what messages (and from whom) they want landing in their inbox, communicating "what's in it for them" is no longer an option.
By setting yourself apart and conveying clearly the value people can expect to receive when they sign up, your organization has a huge opportunity to significantly improve your email acquisition rates today!

experiments.title: How will a direct request in a subject line affect click rates?

rows.name: A surprise for Dr. Carson's birthday
Will you help me celebrate Dr. Carson's birthday?

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a direct request in a subject line will improve click-through rates for a housefile send when compared to a less direct subject line.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/08/2022 - 09/22/2022
In a recent housefile email send promoting the opportunity for subscribers to sign a birthday card for Dr. Ben Carson, we wanted to test how a direct request subject line that puts the call to action front and center affects open rates. We tested this against a subject line that was less direct in mentioning a “birthday surprise,” and made no other changes to the email. We sent each subject line to 50% of the house file to assess the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Including a direct request in the subject line resulted in a 4.5% lift in click rates with a 96.6% level of confidence. We believe this lift occurred because the direct request better aligned recipient expectations as to what the email is all about.

The direct request version of the subject line makes it clear that the recipient is being asked to help celebrate Dr. Ben Carson's birthday in some way while the less direct subject line merely mentions a "surprise," without alluding to the fact that it in some way involves the recipient.

As a result, the recipients who received the direct request subject line were more primed to "help" and thus more likely to click through on a call to action that matches this expectation. Recipients of the less direct subject line may have thought they were simply being informed of a surprise, making them less primed to participate in some way.

Humans crave consistency and this test suggests that by setting an early expectation and keeping that expectation consistent through to the CTA, you are more likely to see engagement with the offer being promoted.

experiments.title: How adding a “give monthly” button to the main navigation menu affects recurring donation rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a give monthly button to a site’s main navigation will achieve higher recurring donation rates .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/23/2022
To increase recurring donation rates for a public policy client, we wanted to know how adding a “give monthly” button to the main navigation menu would impact recurring monthly donation rates. Our hypothesis was that by increasing the visibility of this donation option, more site visitors would decide to give recurring gifts. On desktop this treatment appeared top-of-page, in line with the navigation menu items. On mobile, the give Monthly button was placed at the bottom of a burger menu, directly under the pre-existing donation button.
We ran this experiment for one month, splitting traffic 50/50 between the control and treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment produced a few interesting and unexpected results. First, both the control and treatment (w/ "give monthly" button) failed to produce any recurring gifts, indicating that simply increasing the visibility of the option to make a recurring gift was not enough to motivate prospective donors to do so without a compelling reason why.
Second, while there was not a significant increase in one-time donations for desktop traffic, we did see a valid 144% lift in one-time donations for mobile visitors.
Reviewing these results, we hypothesized that adding a second button increased the visibility of both buttons, not just one. And so we wondered what was causing donors to exclusively choose to give once rather than monthly.
This led us to hypothesize that this increase in one-time giving may have been prompted by the persuasion principle of "reject and retreat". This principle of behavioral psychology states that by "rejecting" a larger ask (a recurring gift), a prospective donor may be more likely to convert when we "retreat" to a smaller commitment (one-time donation).
In this case, by showing two donation options (one representing a larger commitment than the other), donors may have been prompted to reject the larger commitment but then agree to the smaller ask. This may help explain the motivation of choosing one button over the other while so significantly increasing overall giving among people who saw the two-button treatment.
Unfortunately, it is not possible to draw a definitive conclusion one way or the other in this instance, however, this experiment has given us some interesting data points and things to consider about the motivations behind these results.
Since we have discovered that simply adding a monthly donation button provides insufficient motivation for more people to give monthly, we would like to look for future opportunities to increase the visibility of monthly giving, while also conveying why monthly giving is so important and the impact it will have. A sticky bar may be an option, a dear reader, or an exit-intent pop-up. We may also consider drawing attention to the give monthly option + impact on the main donation page. In order to determine the impact of "reject and retreat" on donor motivation, we would need to formulate a more intentional experiment that first asked donors to make a monthly gift, and then followed up with a one-time ask to 50% of traffic. This may be best tested in the form of an appeal that asks specifically for monthly gifts but then follows up with a "retreat" that asks for a one-time gift instead. This might also be tested using a pop-up or some other form of notification. While this may have played a part in the results seen in this experiment, there is simply not enough context to know for sure. But this is an interesting concept that will be worth testing in a different environment.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and urgency on a landing page impacted survey submissions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof and urgency toward a specific goal for survey takers will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/11/2022 - 05/12/2022
ACI was running a survey with high volume but a lower than ideal conversion rate. We evaluated the page for opportunities to increase email acquisition. The first treatment we developed included the element of social proof and let people know that over 55,000 other people had already submitted the survey to increase legitimacy of the offer. We visually highlighted this with a live counter at the top of the page. For the second treatment, we added language to let visitors know we had a goal of 100,000 submissions and their survey response would count toward that goal. We also emphasized this with a progress bar at the top of the page and asked for their help in getting closer to reaching the goal.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for the experiment for one month, when looking at overall traffic, the second treatment had a 3.4% lift in email acquisition with an 81.9% level of confidence. However, when we assessed mobile traffic specifically, we saw a 6.4% directional increase in email acquisition with 94.1% level of confidence on the first treatment and a valid 9.1% increase in email acquisition with 99.3% level of confidence. Additionally, when we isolated traffic by new visitors we also had a 4.9% directional increase in email acquisition with a 91% level of confidence. We believe this was due to the increase in urgency and motivation by highlighting the progress towards the goal on the landing page for page visitors. Because mobile visitors make up almost half of the traffic to the page and neither treatment was showing a decline for overall traffic, we will move forward with rolling out the second treatment to 100% of traffic for future survey takers. For future testing, we could also increase urgency on the landing page by adding a countdown timer or by indicating a specific deadline in the copy to reach 100K responses.

experiments.keyLearnings:
When analyzing the results two days after the initial text went out to the prospective audience, we found that the recurring-specific donation page was able to acquire 222% more recurring donors than the more general page. However, this increase did come at a cost:
The instant donor revenue was $2.5K less than the control versionWe acquired 18.6% less overall donors by asking specifically for recurring (with a 97% statistical level of confidence)
The decrease in donors is not a significant concern since the total number of gifts would balance out by the next month due to the additional recurring donors. However, the reduction of revenue is of note since it primarily came from a decrease in the number of $250 instant gifts.
In terms of the revenue, the instant and downstream ROI of the treatment will balance out after 4 months and would become a net positive in subsequent months from the $1,100 in monthly recurring revenue that was acquired. Overall, it was a successful experiment.
Based upon these learnings, the next best step would be to iterate this experiment by adding in the upper donation amounts back into the gift array and running the split test again. The goal would be to see if we can maintain the recurring gift acquisition rate while not losing some of the larger donors.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).
				This experiment produced the following results:

				× 222.1% increase in conversion rate
				× 0% increase in average gift

experiments.title: How a monthly first ask on a main donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: A monthly first ask on the main donation page will increase recurring giving with minimal impact on overall transactions, as it highlights the ease and effect of becoming a monthly supporter. Meanwhile, the preselected option subtly reinforces that most donors choose to give monthly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/19/2025 - 10/07/2025
For the American Cornerstone Institute, our primary focus has been on growing their recurring donor base. To build on that momentum, we tested adding a recurring gift ask directly on the main donation page, inspired by strong results from earlier acquisition offer tests using a similar approach.
This A/B test ran for just over a month, with equal traffic distributed between the control and treatment versions.
The control utilized the existing donation page without any modifications. The treatment mirrored the control’s copy but added details about the benefits of becoming a monthly partner and preselected the monthly giving option on the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1. Adding a monthly first ask on the main donation page led to a significant increase in recurring donations, with a 223.0% increase in recurring donations for All Traffic. This indicates that highlighting the benefits of becoming a monthly supporter and preselecting the monthly giving option can effectively encourage more donors to choose recurring donations.
2. The treatment also resulted in a substantial increase in overall Transactions, with a 543% increase. This shows that while the focus was on increasing recurring donations, the strategy also had a positive impact on overall donation transactions.
3. The statistical confidence level of 93.6% for the increase in recurring donations and 95% for the rise in transactions suggests that the results are approaching validity, but further testing would be needed for confirmation.
In future experimentation, we should:
1. Continue to explore the impact of emphasizing recurring donations on the main donation page. Further testing can help us validate the results and assess the long-term impact on donor retention and revenue.
2. Consider refining the messaging and design of the monthly giving ask to optimize its effectiveness. This could involve A/B testing different languages, visuals, or incentives to increase recurring donations further.
3. Monitor the overall impact on donation transactions to ensure that the focus on recurring donations does not negatively affect one-time contributions. Finding the right balance between the two types of giving is essential for maximizing fundraising outcomes.
Overall, these findings provide valuable insights into the potential of strategic messaging and design changes to increase recurring donations. By analyzing the results and refining our approach, we can continue to enhance our fundraising strategies and foster sustainable support for the American Cornerstone Institute.

experiments.title: How using donor-centric language on a campaign donation page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using donor-centric language on a campaign donation page for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it makes it more personalized to the donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2023 - 12/01/2023
This organization wanted to test how using donor-centric language on their donation page for Giving Tuesday affected conversion rates overall. Traffic came to this page through paid Facebook ads, housefile emails and website traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using donor-centric language on a campaign donation page will likely lead to a higher conversion rate. The treatment group, which received the donor-centric language, saw a 23.3% increase in transactions compared to the control group with an 88% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment version saw an 9% increase in revenue with a 91% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue testing the use of donor-centric language and other personalization strategies to optimize the donation page. It would be beneficial to run longer experiments to gather more data and improve the statistical validity of the results. Additionally, testing different variations of donor-centric language and comparing them against each other can help identify the most effective messaging.

experiments.title: How using a credible figure to talk in first person on a donation page affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a donation page like its a letter from an important figure for potential donors will achieve a increase conversion rate because it adds credibility and trust to the process. because  it adds credibility and trust to the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2023 - 12/14/2023
As apart of ACI’s calendar year-end campaign, they sent a survey to their housefile asking for feedback on how the organization is doing. After you complete the survey, you go to an instant donation page that includes value proposition on making a donation before the end of the year. ACI’s founder, Dr. Ben Carson, is a huge voice in the organization and to its followers, so we decided to test writing the donation page as if it were a letter from him.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using language that presents the donation page as a letter from an important figure, such as Dr. Ben Carson in this case, can cause an increase the conversion rate. The results of the experiment showed a 29.6% increase in transactions for the treatment version with a statistical level of confidence of 83.4%.
Because we saw a directional lift in conversion rate, we believe that Dr. Carson writing the donation page as a letter does increase credibility and trust with the organization and the user.

experiments.title: How using priming language on a landing page affects email conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe the treatment page using “priming” will increase the email sign-up rate for the video series because it primes visitors to associate positive emotions and a sense of warmth with ‘The Inklings’ and the video series, making them more inclined to take action by signing up for access.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/18/2024 - 01/26/2024
Hillsdale College is offering a free video series on “The Inklings” through paid Facebook advertising. They wanted to test how using the psychological concept “priming” would affect email conversion on the page to sign up to get the video series. On the treatment page, the priming language helps associate the video series with positive emotions and a sense of warmth like the Inklings experienced when they were discussing literature and theology.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment page using "priming" actually resulted in a decrease in email sign-up rate for the video series. This means that the hypothesis that priming visitors with positive emotions and a sense of warmth would increase their likelihood of signing up for access was incorrect.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of priming on the desired outcome. While priming can be a powerful psychological tool, it is not always effective in achieving the desired results. This experiment suggests that in this particular case, priming may have had a negative effect on the decision to sign up for the video series.
Therefore, it would be beneficial to explore other strategies and messaging techniques that could potentially increase email acquisition. This could involve testing different types of language, imagery, or incentives to better appeal to the target audience. Additionally, conducting further research to gain a deeper understanding of the target audience's motivations and preferences could also inform future experimentation and optimize the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How the way we address page visitors once they arrive to the donation page impact donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that addressing page visitors as “Friend” or by their first name will achieve an increase in donations because page visitors will appreciate the personal touch of a salutation and feel like they’re being spoken to conversationally rather than just visiting a regular old web page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/15/2023 - 07/03/2023
ACI’s Cancel Culture donation page is producing a low instant donor conversion rate compared to their other offers. We wondered if the way we address donation page visitors once they arrive on the page would impact donations.
We separated this test into a, b, and c variant.
The “a” variant is a regular headline
The “b” variant implements “Friend” in the headline
And the “c” variant implements the user’s first name in the headline
We want to see if either the “Friend” or first name headlines move the needle in terms of increasing donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 15 days, each treatment did produce an increase in donor conversion rate compared to the control. Treatment 1 (Friend acknowledgement) produced a 28.9% increase with a 63.1% level of confidence, while treatment 2 (first name acknowledgement) produced a 42.4% increase with an 80% level of confidence.
Treatment 2 also produced a 20.6% increase in revenue with a 69% level of confidence.
It's clear that our donation page visitors prefer a personal acknowledgement over no acknowledgement at all.
Interestingly, new visitors led to the highest increase in donations when being addressed as "Friend". This segment resulted in a 64% increase with a nearly valid 83.1% level of confidence. Our existing visitors responded best to the first name acknowledgement.
We plan to run a new a/b test as our next iteration testing "Friend" vs First Name against each other, eliminating the current control, to see which generates the greater increase in donor conversion. This test should be able to run for the fully recommended 30 days to see if either variant reaches a valid result.
It will be interesting to see if we see the same trend in the results for the way new visitors and existing visitors are being addressed when we run the a/b test.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition language to a main donation page affects donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition language on the main donation page will increase donations by providing more “reasons to give” to prospective donors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/14/2022 - 01/21/2023
In an effort to increase donation rates on a public policy organization’s main donation page, we wanted to test adding more value prop language to further convey the value of a visitor’s donation by improving the “reason why” they should give.
This test was run only on the “main” donation page, i.e., not a donation page associated with a specific acquisition effort, which is generally reached by clicking on a “make a donation” button on the main navigation bar of the site.
We designed this experiment as an A/B test, with roughly 50% of the traffic sent to the control and roughly 50% of the traffic redirected to the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, we observed 12.5% decrease in donations from traffic that was redirected to the treatment donation page with more value proposition language. The low sample size of the experiment prevented us from validating these results, however, it does not appear that adding more value proposition language to the main donation page motivated more people to give and may even have demotivated visitors from giving.
And while we cannot draw statistically valid conclusions about these results, it is worth noting that this outcome may have been influenced by user motivation. Specifically, after reviewing results, we are led to hypothesize that because someone who clicks a "make a donation" button on a website has a high motivation to give, increasing the length and content of the donation page language may create unnecessary friction to the donation process by presenting an overlong wall of copy for them to read. In other words, we suspect that when a potential donor is sufficiently motivated to give, it may be best to "stay out of their way" by using less copy which is more generalized above the donation form.
This suspicion is supported by the fact that "new visitors" who we may assume would be the exact segment that would benefit most from increasing the "reason to give" gave 28% less (86% level of confidence) when visiting the treatment with longer copy than they did when they visited the control with minimal copy above the donation form.

experiments.title: How presenting a daily goal in an email appeal affects donations during a year-end fundraising campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a daily goal in the body of an email appeal will increase donation rates by increasing the specificity of the ask and giving recipients a clear ‘micro’ goal that they can help achieve.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/27/2022
For this experiment, we wanted to test how presenting a daily goal (“today I’m looking for X donors”) in the body copy of an email appeal would affect donation rates for a public policy organization during a year-end fundraising campaign.
For this experiment, both previous donors and non-donors were included in the sample universe with ~50% of appeal recipients receiving the control (containing an ask that did not include a daily goal) and ~50% of appeal recipients receiving the treatment (containing an ask centered around a daily goal. Nothing else about the appeal was changed.
Major donors were excluded from this test and our experiment spanned seven appeal emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment over the course of seven emails during the organization's year-end fundraising campaign, we observed that the treatment produced a 12.2% decrease in donations (84% level of confidence).
The sample size of this experiment did not allow us to validate these results and therefore prevents us from drawing statistically valid conclusions about how presenting a daily goal in the body of an appeal email affects donation rates.
However, the results of this experiment do suggest that including a daily goal was not a motivating factor for recipients of this appeal and may even have demotivated them from giving. We also observed that the daily goal did not serve as a significant motivating factor in terms of clicking through to the donation page from the email, producing only a 2.1% lift with a 53.6% level of statistical confidence.
This may be because a daily goal does not add tangible value or a reason to give to the appeal. It is also in essence an organizational-focused goal that lacks a specific outcome that the daily goal will help achieve.
Based on these results, this public policy client may want to pursue a different method for increasing engagement and donations on the next high-urgency campaign or consider including a specific outcome that the daily goal will achieve. In either case, the organization should not assume that including a daily goal, as structured in this experiment, will increase donation rates during a high-urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How a premium impacted donor convertion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a book premium for survey takers from the email house file will achieve increase donor acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2021 - 11/18/2021
American Cornerstone Institute wanted to find new ways to incentivize their email house file to make a donation. As part of their education survey instant donation page, they decided to test out including a free copy of the Little Patriots first children’s book with a donation of $25 or more. Since the donor audience is a bit older, the book was framed as a great way for them to impart this same curriculum to their children or grandchildren.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After receiving over 150 donations, we found that the book premium did not improve statistically donor conversion--it actually seemed to have a negative impact (although that was not statistically significant.) Since this premium had incremental costs associated with it, we decided to end the experiment prior to it validating.
With an average instant donor conversion rate of 18.5%, the next logical step is to find ways to increase the value received from the donors rather than trying to getting more of them to give. The average instant donor conversion rate is normally between 1% to 4%. With this in mind, the next experiment will focus in increasing recurring donor acquisition.

experiments.title: How merging two appeals together impacted donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that intertwining messaging and value proposition around Dr. Carson’s birthday card and the Little Patriots appeal for ACI Housefile subscribers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it ties the celebration of Dr. Carson directly to advancing the mission of ACI, allowing donors to honor him in a more meaningful and impactful way.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/29/2025 - 09/26/2025
During ACI’s Fall High Urgency Campaign, we included both an appeal around Dr. Carson’s birthday and an appeal to expand the Little Patriots curriculum nationwide.
To add value for the donor, we tested a combined version for the ACI housefile, weaving the ask for Little Patriots into Dr. Carson’s birthday card appeal. This allowed us to see whether a unified message could deepen engagement and increase response
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
1.The treatment, which intertwined messaging around Dr. Carson's birthday card and the Little Patriots appeal, saw a 5.2% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control group.
2. The statistical confidence level for this result was only 24.9%, which is below the 95% threshold for validity.
3. Due to the low statistical confidence level, this experiment's results should not be used for future decision-making.
4. It is essential to ensure that future experiments have a higher level of statistical confidence before drawing any conclusions or making decisions based on the results. 5. While the initial results show potential for a positive impact on donor conversion by linking Dr. Carson's work with the Little Patriots program, further testing with a larger sample size or different methodology may be necessary to confirm this potential impact.

experiments.title: How the addition of a goal thermometer impacts email conversion rate on funnel landing page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the introduction of a goal amount in the copy and a goal thermometer for landing page visitors will achieve an increase in email conversion rate because it reinforces social proof and urgency on the page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/02/2025 - 04/17/2025
In an effort to boost email conversions on ACI’s Landing page, we opted to test into a goal thermometer on the page to focus in on urgency and social proof of joining a community of similarly minded individuals.
We have tested this concept on different ACI funnels and seen positive results, so we were hopeful to see the same for the landing page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which introduced a goal amount in the copy and a goal thermometer, resulted in a 0.6% decrease in Email Acquisition for All Traffic with a 21.9% statistical level of confidence. This means that the control version, without the goal amount and thermometer, performed better than the treatment.

However, since the statistical level of confidence is below 95%, the results cannot be considered valid or reliable for making future decisions.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to provide statistically significant results with a high level of confidence. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test variations of the treatment to see if different approaches to presenting the goal amount and thermometer could yield more positive results.

Overall, the key takeaway is to always validate results with a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing any changes based on an A/B experiment.

experiments.title: How including the gift array in a sticky bar affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a gift array on a sticky bar for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rates because it simplifies the donation process, reduces friction by allowing users to select a donation amount directly from the sticky bar, and leverages psychological principles such as pre-commitment and anchoring..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2024 - 12/27/2024
The American Cornerstone Institute ran a high-urgency campaign for the final month of the year. Within this campaign, they wanted to test the impact of including the gift array on the sticky bar of their website.
They conducted an A/B test, split 50/50, with two versions of the sticky bar: the control version was a standard sticky bar with a button to take them to the donation page and the treatment version included the gift array in the sticky bar which took them to the donation page and pre-selected the amount they chose on the sticky bar.
Their hypothesis was that the gift array would increase donor conversion rate because it simplifies the donation process, reduces friction by allowing users to select a donation amount directly from the sticky bar, and leverages psychological principles such as pre-commitment and anchoring.
By making the options immediately visible and actionable, we anticipate that users will be more engaged and motivated to complete their donation, leading to higher conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:

1. Including a gift array on the sticky bar did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rates. In fact, the treatment version saw a 0.3% decrease in donations for all traffic.

2. The statistical level of confidence was only 0.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This indicates that the results of the experiment are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to:

- Ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. In this case, the experiment ran for 20 days, but it may have been beneficial to run it for a longer period or with a larger sample size.

- Consider other factors that may influence donor conversion rates, such as the placement of the sticky bar on the website, the wording and language used in the sticky bar, and the overall user experience on the donation page.

- Continue to test different strategies and elements on the website to optimize donor conversion rates. While this experiment did not yield the desired results, it is important to learn from it and apply those learnings to future experiments to improve the effectiveness of donation strategies.

experiments.title: How a personal ask to match a gift amount affects overall conversions in an email appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personal ask to match a gift for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because because it creates a sense of urgency and personal connection, motivating donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, especially when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
American Cornerstone Institute wanted to test how a more personal approach to asking to match a gift during year-end would affect overall conversion rates including clickthrough rate from the email to donation page and donation conversion rate.
They tested this concept within three emails they sent throughout their year-end campaign. This write-up shows the results of email 7 which was an appeal for education.
The control email presented the matching gift by stating that their gift would be doubled thanks to their year-end matching gift challenge up to $100,000. The treatment email presented the matching gift by stating that a generous donor wants to personally match their gift allowing them to have double the impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that a more personal approach to asking for a matching gift can potentially lead to an increase in conversion rates. The treatment email, which presented the matching gift as being from a generous donor wanting to personally match the contribution, resulted in a 33.7% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control email.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was 77.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

This suggests that creating a sense of urgency and personal connection in donation appeals can motivate potential donors to feel their contribution has a direct and meaningful impact, particularly when paired with the opportunity to double their support through matching.

As for the effect on email click-through rate, the treatment email saw no difference in click-through rate. There was a 1.9% increase in clicks with only a 21% level of confidence.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of a personal ask for matching gifts with a larger sample size to see if the trend towards increased conversion rates holds true. Additionally, considering other variables such as the timing of the ask, the messaging used, and the target audience could provide further insights into what drives donor behavior and how to optimize fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How using a countdown clock on a donation page in the final days of a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown clock to the donation page will increase the donation conversion rate because it creates urgency by highlighting the time remaining until the deadline.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2024 - 01/01/2025
The American Cornerstone Institute ran a high-urgency campaign for the final month of the year. Within this campaign, they wanted to test the impact of including a countdown clock on the donation for the final 4 days of the campaign.
They conducted an A/B test, split 50/50, with two versions of the donation page: the control version without a countdown clock and the treatment version with a countdown clock. Their hypothesis was that the countdown clock would create a sense of urgency, encouraging more users to make a donation before the deadline (When the countdown clock ran out).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the inclusion of a countdown clock on the donation page did not have the intended impact of increasing donation conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group, which had the countdown clock, experienced a 6.6% decrease in transactions compared to the control group.

It is crucial to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 59.0%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the statistical significance of the results before drawing any conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the effectiveness of different strategies to create urgency or incentivize donations, such as limited-time offers or matching donation challenges, to see what resonates best with donors. Conducting further A/B tests with a larger sample size and varying durations may provide more conclusive insights for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding credibility to a petition landing page impact email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that writing a landing page like its a letter from an important figure for potential Pro-Life Petition signers will achieve an increase in email acquistion because it adds credibility and trust to the process.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/25/2024
In an effort to boost the email acquisition rate on ACI’s Pro-Life Petition landing page, we decided to run an A/B Test to lean into additional credibility through the presences of Dr. Ben Carson.
The treatment landing page copy was written as a letter from Dr. Ben Carson along with his signature.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which involved presenting the landing page as a letter from Dr. Ben Carson, did not result in an increase in email acquisition for the Pro-Life Petition. In fact, there was a slight decrease in emails acquired compared to the control group.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment do not have a statistically significant level of confidence, as the confidence level is below 95%. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further refine the treatment and perhaps test different variables to determine what truly resonates with potential petition signers. It is also important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide more reliable results with a higher level of confidence. Additionally, considering the sensitivity of the topic, it may be valuable to approach the messaging and presentation of the petition in a thoughtful and strategic manner to effectively engage the target audience.

experiments.title: How adding a phone number field to a donation form impacts transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a phone number field to the donation page for donation page vistors will achieve a similar amount of donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2024 - 08/09/2024
We wanted to start sending a thank you voicemail to donors after they donated, but to be able to do this we needed to start collecting phone numbers on the donation page. Before rolling out this change to the donation page, we wanted to make sure it wouldn’t impact the amount of transactions on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a phone number field to the donation page resulted in a 7.3% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 56.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of the experiment cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to provide reliable and statistically significant results. Additionally, it is crucial to adhere to a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing any changes based on an experiment. This will help to avoid making decisions that may not actually have a meaningful impact on the desired outcome.

experiments.title: How a lower gift array impacts transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that lowering the gift array options for donation page visitors will achieve more transactions because the amount we are asking for is less.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/17/2024 - 08/09/2024
We were running ads for an Education Survey on Facebook for ACI and noticed that people were selecting the two lower options in the gift array, people weren’t selecting the two higher options. We decided to test a lower gift array to try to increase the amount of transactions on the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that lowering the gift array options for donation page visitors resulted in a 3.0% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 20.5%, meaning that these results cannot be considered valid. As a result, we should not use these findings for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to obtain statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be helpful to consider other factors that could impact transaction rates, such as the appeal of the donation page, the target audience, and the effectiveness of the ad campaign. By taking these factors into account and conducting well-designed experiments with higher levels of confidence, we can gather more reliable insights to inform future strategies for increasing transactions on the donation page.

experiments.title: How reinforcing the impact of a gift impacted transactions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including this statement of value proposition for visitors will achieve an increase in the number of transactions because the visitor will be reminded about the impact that their gift could make within the donation form.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/18/2023 - 11/27/2023
In an effort to increase the number of donations for ACI, we wanted to test adding additional value proposition within the donation form. We tested this on a multitude of pages with varying content and offers ranging from the homepage donation page, pro-life pledge donation page, education survey donation page, and a few others. With this being said, each different topic had a different value proposition statement that was relevant. Here are the statements that we used:
Pro-life: Your donation today will help fight for the rights of unborn children across the nation.
Education: Your ongoing support will help educate thousands of children on the true principles of America’s founding throughout the year.
General: Your recurring monthly gift will defend our nation’s guiding principles of life, liberty, community, and faith 365 days a year.
Little Patriots: The entirety of your gift will directly support our efforts to provide free curricula and cartoons that teach true American history through our Little Patriots program.
This statement was shown under the tabbed options of “One-time” or “Monthly,” and it was shown regardless of which tab the visitor had selected.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a value proposition statement within the donation form had a slightly positive impact on the conversion rate for transactions at 4.9%.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for these results is only 67.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further test the impact of value proposition statements on donation conversions, using larger sample sizes and longer experiment durations. Additionally, it would be valuable to determine whether the recurring specific statements were more or less beneficial in the future.

By conducting more rigorous experiments and increasing the statistical level of confidence, we can obtain more reliable and actionable insights on how to optimize the donation form and increase the number of donations for the American Cornerstone Institute.

experiments.title: How shortening copy on a donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that shortening the copy for instant donation page visitors will achieve a higher instant conversion rate because the donor will be able to see the donation form right away, which takes away friction.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/15/2024 - 07/05/2024
ACI has a survey that has been running in acquisition for a while. It was preforming well, but we were wanting to refresh the copy. We decided to test the donation page, because the copy on it was long. We tested shorter copy, so it all could fit on the page without the user having to scroll. That way, when the user arrives on the page, they can see the donation form.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that shortening the copy on the instant donation page did not lead to an increase in conversion rate for one-time donations. The treatment group actually experienced a 6.3% decrease in conversion rate compared to the control group.

Since the statistical confidence level of 37.0% is below the standard threshold of 95%, these results cannot be considered reliable or valid for making future decisions.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that sample sizes are sufficient to achieve a statistically significant result before drawing conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test different variables or elements on the donation page to see what resonates best with donors. By continuing to experiment and gather data, we can make more informed decisions on how to optimize the donation page for better conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a donation page impacts donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a quote from former president Trump about Dr. Carson for donation page vistors will achieve more transactions because there is social proof that what ACI does is approved by Trump.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/18/2024 - 05/15/2024
ACI’s Dr. Carson has a relationship with former president Trump. Since ACI’s audience is mainly conservatives, we wanted to try using a quote from Trump about Dr. Carson on a few of ACI’s instant donation pages. We created a sticky bar that had the quote and a photo of Trump.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a quote from former president Trump about Dr. Carson on the donation page did not result in an increase in transactions. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in recurring donations compared to the control group, although the statistical significance was only 38.9%.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to provide valid and reliable results. Additionally, it is crucial to consider the audience and their preferences when incorporating endorsements or quotes from public figures. It may be beneficial to test different messaging strategies or elements to understand what resonates best with ACI's conservative audience. A larger sample size and further testing could provide more conclusive insights for future decision-making.

experiments.title: How reinforcing a pledge agreement impacted conversion on an instant donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that reinforcing the agreement to the pledge on the instant donation page will cause an increase in donor conversion rate because of its emphasis on personal agreement, and detailed actions to combat cancel culture. The explicit call for agreement and clear steps for support may enhance engagement, making the treatment more effective in motivating individuals to donate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/09/2024 - 03/25/2024
When looking to improve conversion on an instant donation page behind a pledge, we wondered if including additional emphasis on personal agreement and detailed actions to combat cancel culture would lead to higher participation on the donation page. We developed a treatment that reminded visitors of their shared commitment in the same space as asking them to make a gift to combat the issue they stood against. We split traffic 50/50 and ran the experiment for six weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which included reinforcing the agreement to the pledge on the instant donation page with detailed actions to combat cancel culture, did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment saw a 4.2% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control group. We believe this may be due to additional friction because of the length of the copy.

However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 22.7%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. This means that the results of this experiment cannot be considered reliable or used for future decision making.

It may be beneficial in future tests to consider revisiting the design and messaging of the treatment to see if any adjustments can be made to improve its effectiveness in driving donor conversions. Additionally, testing different variables or approaches in future experiments can help in understanding what resonates best with donors and ultimately leads to higher conversion rates.

experiments.title: How donor first party data through AVID Pathways impacted Facebook match rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that AVID Pathways connection  for AVID clients will achieve a higher match rate in Facebook because AVID has access to multiple sources of data. because  AVID has access to multiple sources of data.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/28/2024
With the introduction of AVID Pathways, we decided to test the Facebook match rate of contacts uploaded via CSV versus that of a continuous AVID Pathways connection.
For the control of this experiment, we exported donors from the organization’s CRM and uploaded the list to Facebook as an audience. The data points provided included first name, last name, email address, phone number, city, state, and zip code.
For the treatment of this experiment, we created a segment of donors in AVID Pathways and then pushed that segment into Facebook as an audience. The AVID Pathways connection to Facebook is a continuous flow of information from the organization’s platforms, such as CRM, email, and donation platforms combined.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the AVID Pathways connection for AVID clients showed a slight increase in match rate compared to the traditional CSV upload method. While this result suggests that there may be potential benefits to utilizing AVID Pathways for connecting with donors on Facebook, the statistical level of confidence at 15.9% is not strong enough to draw conclusive findings.
For this experiment, we utilized 2 different segments of donors based on the giving cohort:
Offline only donorsOnline only donors
For each segment, we saw the following results.
For offline donors, we saw a 1.7% decrease in match rate with a level of confidence at 82%For only donors, we saw a 1.2% decrease in match rate with a level of confidence at 99%
While these segments did see very slight decreases, we are confident that the continuous flow of information provide via AVID Pathways from multiple data sources will make up the decrease.
As AVID Pathways data continues to improve we expect these match rates to increase over time.
We will check back in on rate in a few months to confirm if match rates have improved.

experiments.title: How including an image of a premium in an email impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including an image of the premium for email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because there is greater clarity about the children’s book and its intended audience.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
When offering to send a book as a premium for gifts of a certain amount, we wondered if having an image of the book being offered would have any impact on email engagement by generating interest and curiosity about the contents of the book as well as creating congruency between the email and the donation page, which also had the image of the book displayed at the point of transaction. Since the author also writes books for adults, we wanted to provide clarity at the point of decision-making that this book would be best suited for young readers or any children in the visitors’ lives.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including an image of the premium in email communications might have a positive impact on engagement and click-through rates. Although the treatment group did see a 7.1% increase in clicks, the statistical confidence level of 64.8% is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the inclusion of visuals, such as images of premiums, in email communications to gauge their impact on recipient engagement. However, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence in the results. Additionally, incorporating other elements such as A/B testing different visuals or messaging strategies could provide further insights into what resonates best with the audience and drives the desired actions.

experiments.title: How adding clarity around the impact of a donation affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity about the impact of their gift and the matching gift opportunity for subscribers will achieve an increase in donor conversion because  it provides more clarity and transparency, addressing potential concerns and building trust.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/14/2023
ACI is running their CYE campaign. We wanted to test in the pro-life-focused email how adding clarity about the impact of their gift and the impact of the matching gift opportunity would affect the conversion rate. We did this by adding specific bullets about what their gift will do to help move ACI’s mission forward.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding clarity about the impact of their gift and the matching gift opportunity in the pro-life-focused email had a negative effect on the donor conversion rate. The treatment group, which received the email with the added clarity, saw a 21.2% decrease in donations compared to the control group.
However, it is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, as the level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 85%.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct a more robust study with a larger sample size and a longer testing period to gather more reliable results. Additionally, it may be worth exploring different variations of the email content to see if there are other messaging strategies that can effectively communicate the impact of their gift and the matching gift opportunity without negatively affecting the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How using language around the impact of the matching gift affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using language around the impact of the matching gift rather than simply stating it will be doubled  for ACI’s housefile will achieve an increase in conversion rate  because it provides more clarity and transparency, addressing potential concerns and building trust. because  it provides more clarity and transparency, addressing potential concerns and building trust.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/14/2023
ACI is running their CYE campaign. They have a $100,000 matching grant for this campaign, so we wondered how using language around the impact of the matching gift rather than just saying their gift will have double the impact would affect donor conversion rate. We ran this test in an email to their full housefile.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using language around the impact of a matching gift instead of simply stating that the gift will be doubled did not have an effect on donor conversion rate.
Because CYE is already a motivating time to give, we believe that the added context and clarity did not increase motivation to give because it was already so high.
In future experimentation, it would be wise to consider alternative approaches to communicate the impact of a matching gift. It may be beneficial to test different messaging strategies, such as emphasizing the urgency or importance of the campaign or highlighting specific examples of how the matching gift will be used to make a difference.

experiments.title: How a recurring donation interrupter affects recurring donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift for potential donors will achieve a higher recurring donor conversion rate because they will be forced to think of the impact and what they receive in return to convert the one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/07/2023 - 01/05/2024
MSLF is running a petition through paid Facebook advertising, and they wanted to test how a donation pop-up interrupter would perform to increase the amount of recurring donors they get through acquisition efforts.
Once users finish the petition, they proceed to an instant donation page where they’re asked to make a gift to support this case and the work MSLF is doing. There are options to make a one-time or monthly gift on the donation form, but if the user chose one-time, they would receive a pop-up after hitting “Donate” asking them to convert their gift to a $10 monthly donation.
The pop-up included value proposition as to what they will receive if they make a monthly donation and become a member to MSLF.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a donation interrupter to ask users to convert their one-time gift to a monthly gift can significantly increase the conversion rate for recurring donations.
The treatment group experienced a 154.5% increase in recurring donation conversion rate, indicating that the interrupter was effective in prompting users to consider the impact of their donation and what they would receive in return as a member of the organization. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 75.1%, which falls below the recommended confidence level of 85%.
In addition, the experiment showed that the one-time donation rate decreased by 326% with a 97% level of confidence. This means that the interrupter influenced users to opt out of giving all together instead of making a one-time gift or recurring gift.
Future experimentation can involve refining the design and messaging of the interrupter to maximize its impact. It is also important to consider the potential impact of the interrupter on user experience and whether it could potentially deter users from making any donation at all. A/B testing with different variations of the interrupter can help in identifying the most effective approach.
Overall, the key learnings from this experiment highlight the potential of using a donation interrupter to increase recurring donations. However, further experimentation and validation are needed to make informed decisions and implement effective strategies.

experiments.title: How increasing the perceived security on a campaign donation page impacts donation conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a statement of security for the year end campaign donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion because it is providing clarity of what the donor’s information is being used for.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/12/2023 - 01/04/2024
Luther Seminary ran a fundraising campaign during Calendar Year End. For this campaign, we wanted to see how increasing the perceived security of the donation page through value proposition impacted overall donor conversions.
For this test, we added a line of copy below the “Donate” button on the form that said, “We take data and privacy seriously. Your payment information is secure and will be used only to process your gift.” We also made the background of this section gray and added a lock icon to drive the secure message home.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a statement of security to the donation page for the year-end campaign resulted in a 14.2% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 62.5%, which means that these results cannot be considered valid or reliable for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to increase the sample size and run the experiment for a longer duration to obtain more reliable results. Additionally, it would be beneficial to test other variations of the value proposition statement to further improve donor conversion rates. This could involve testing different wording, design elements, or positioning of the security message. By continuing to experiment and refine the messaging and design of the donation page, it is possible to achieve a higher level of donor conversion and improve the overall success of fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding privacy language below a survey sign up submit button impacted sign up rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a more formal privacy statement at the below the survey submit button will increase the education survey sign up rate because it will establish more credibility and trust in ACI and the survey itself.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2023 - 06/24/2023
ACI’s Education Survey is producing a 43.3% sign up rate over the last month. While this metric is performing above-benchmark, we believe there is room for improvement for this offer to match the sign up rates of other offers such as the Cancel Culture Pledge (59.5% sign up rate).
We recently tested applying privacy language below the survey sign up form on the Education Survey landing page, as we’ve seen this tactic have success across other similar organizations. The historical learning is that privacy language builds trust with our audience as many people can feel vulnerable giving away their information, so the implementation of privacy language (your information is secure with us) provides users more comfort as they make the decision to submit their survey and personal information.
Though, our recent test proved unsuccessful, producing a 4.3% drop in email acquisition with a 99% level of confidence. You can review this experiment at this link: https://app.winstonknows.com/e/Ewwm
As a second iteration, we think placing the privacy language below the form AND survey submit button will be the key to seeing a lift in email sign ups – this opposed to our previous experiment which placed the language between the from and the button.
Placing it below the button will avoid disrupting the sign up process and avoid making users think that they should think twice about submitting their information as they get ready to press the submit button below. Placing the text below the button serves more so as a supportive reminder.
Let’s see how our second iteration performs…
experiments.keyLearnings:
Iteration #2 did not produce a valid drop; however, it did produce very flat results. The treatment saw a 0.07% decrease in email acquisition rate with only a 3.51% level of confidence. This means that the data does not support the addition of privacy language being a part of the Education Survey sign up page.
There was no notable lift downstream or through any alternative segments, either.
After two attempts, adding privacy language does not seem to move the needle with our audience. Ultimately, this is a good thing as users seem to already trust the organization and do not need to be told that their information is secure in order to feel comfortable submitting a form.
We should move our experiment brainstorming to another element on the page to see if we can uncover a desired life in email acquisition.

experiments.title: How adding a relevant image to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a relevant image on an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will appreciate the image and gain inspiration from it leading them to make a gift to receive the book.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2023 - 06/03/2023
In an effort to boost instant donor conversion rate through ACI’s Red, White, and Blue direct ask funnel, we want to test adding a relevant image to the donation page body section. The image is of Dr. Ben Carson reading the Red, White, and Blue: Our Flag Matters to Me and You book that he wrote to an audience of American children. We selected this image because it encompasses his image and likeness, his influence and care towards educating and engaging the American youth, and the book he is reading is the book someone can make a donation to receive on this donation page.
We believe that implementing this warm, inspiring, and relatable image as potential donors read through the value proposition will encourage more people to give.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The addition of the image within the value proposition copy did not provide a boost in donor conversion like we hypothesized it would. In fact, the treatment led to a 14% drop in donor conversion with a 68.2% level of confidence. We saw a steeper 22% decrease in revenue with a nearly valid 91% level of confidence.
This is our second attempt at adding an image of Dr. Carson and the Red, White, and Blue book and both attempts led to a decrease in donations. It seems our audience does not respond well to images, or perhaps disruptions, as they are reading the value proposition. They may arrive at the page already motivated and the image could simply get in their way as they're working their way to the donation form. They may arrive at the page and are turned off by the image, as they may associate images with ads or blogs, or something that may turn them off when they're online.
It may be time to transition away from image implementation and try experimenting with other elements of the page.

experiments.title: How a continuation headline + congruent value prop messaging impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a continuation headline and more congruent reasons to give consistent with the theme of the funnel will achieve an increase in donations among donation page visitors.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2023 - 06/04/2023
ACI’s Judicial Weaponization Petition funnel is producing a low instant donor conversion rate compared to similar offers. Click through rate at the ad level and petition sign up rate are very healthy, but we’re struggling to get donors to convert once they reach the instant donation page.
Reviewing the current donation page, two things stood out:
1) The headline comes off as conclusive and does not imply that there is another action the petition signer can take.
2) The reasons to give in the last paragraph are not on theme with the petition subject and therefore could be creating a disconnect that turns the page visitor away.
We developed a treatment that implements a continuation headline and closing value proposition copy (reasons to give) that align with the action they just took (signing a petition to stop judicial weaponization).
Let’s see how the treatment performs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results were not what we anticipated. The treatment resulted in a 6.6% decrease in donations with a 20.1% level of confidence. Revenue to the treatment resulted in an 18% decrease with a 67.6% level of confidence. Ultimately, neither variant moved the needle.
Interestingly, there was a notable difference between mobile and desktop visitors:
Mobile: Treatment produced a 95.1% increase in donations with an 85.7% LoC
Desktop: Treatment produced a 42.5% decrease in donations with an 88.2% LoC
These outcomes came close to validating, however, it's difficult to put a finger on what led to these vastly different user responses given that the copy length stayed roughly the same and there was little difference in the actual visibility of the page.
We'll keep these results in mind and see if we can test this similar approach for other offers. If we do, we'll monitor to see if results are consistent or different to the ones we've recorded here.

experiments.title: How personalizing a donation ask with a signature impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing a donation ask to come from a well-known authority figure and adding his signature at the end of the ask will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will feel called upon directly by the authority figure to make an impact, and the signature will provide authenticity to the ask.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/27/2023 - 05/29/2023
For ACI’s Religious Liberty Survey funnel, we want to test a personalization approach for the offer instant donation page in which we will frame the value proposition to come from the organization’s leading authority figure. To execute this approach, we’ve:
updated the headline to call attention to the following message from the authority figure 
placed the message in quotations to show that it is being spoken/written from the authority figure
added the authority figure’s signature after the message to “authenticate” it
Our hypothesis is that this combination of personalization tactics will resonate more strongly with donation page visitors and lead to an increase in donations.
The control maintains value proposition language that comes from the organization.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, which presented a donation ask from ACI Founder Dr. Ben Carson with his signature added post-message, resulted in an 11.7% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 76.8%, below the required 95% for the results to be considered valid.
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a well-known authority figure's signature to a donation ask can potentially increase donations, but further testing is necessary to validate the results.
In reviewing the treatment, we wonder if the "special offer" text next to Dr. Carson's signature on desktop and below his signature on mobile takes away from the personalization by introducing marketing copy between the message and the donation form. As a supplement test, we may want to experiment with removing or relocating the "special offer" text to where it does not disrupt the momentum the potential donor has going from the value prop to the donation form.

experiments.title: How staging a donation ask to come from an authority figure impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing a donation ask to come from a well-known authority figure for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will feel called upon by the authority figure to make an impact, and this version of a donation ask will hold more value and emphasis. because  potential donors will feel called upon by the authority figure to make an impact, and this version of a donation ask will hold more value and emphasis.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/10/2023 - 05/04/2023
For ACI’s Judicial Weaponization Petition, we want to test two different copy angles to see which one produces the most donations. The control is our conventional approach, consisting value proposition speaking from the organization to the end-user. The treatment take a first person approach, presenting the donation ask from ACI Founder Dr. Ben Carson.
Leveraging Dr. Carson’s name and likeness has worked in many previous tests, so we want to try it for this offer funnel and donation ask.
We want to determine if a message communicated from Dr. Carson will resonate more with potential donors and lead to more donations. Will appreciate the personal message? Will they take action based on the message of “will you join with me?”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that framing a donation ask to come from an authority figure may have a positive impact on donations. The treatment featuring Dr. Ben Carson as the messenger resulted in a 2.6% increase in donations for all traffic. However, since the statistical level of confidence was only 25.5%, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
The very slight life in donations does not lead us to confidently believe that the treatment is the optimal option moving forward.
Revenue resulted in a 5.1% increase with a higher but still invalid 66.5% level of confidence.
For a future test, we may consider emphasizing Dr. Carson in a more unique way, such as through a humanized sticky bar at the top of the page. This would enable users to hear from him even sooner.

experiments.title: How adding a relevant image highlighting an authority figure to an instant donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a relevant image highlighting an authority figure to an instant donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because seeing the authority figure in action and in a natural, relatable manner will make them want to support the organization and receive the premium offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/06/2023 - 04/29/2023
ACI’s Red, White, and Blue book instant donation page is producing a low instant donor conversion rate. This is a direct ask funnel where users go directly from an ad to the donation page, where they can receive this book when making a donation of a certain amount. We wondered if implementing an image of the author of the book and the face of the ACI organization – Dr. Ben Carson – would help improve donor conversion rate. The book is written for children and the image shows Dr. Carson signing the book for a young kid. Will the relevance of the image and the inclusion of Dr. Carson make potential donors appreciate the opportunity more and convince them to make a donation to receive the book?
Let’s find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding an image highlighting an authority figure to an instant donation page may not always result in an increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment (instant donation page WITH the image) resulted in a 7.5% decrease in donations with a 42.8% level of confidence.
Revenue did however produce a 33.7% increase with a 90.5% level of confidence. Additionally, new visitors produced an 86.4% increase in donations with a 78.% LOC compared to returning visitors producing a 5% decrease in donations.
The overall results tell us that there is potential with this concept. Overall donations did not increase like we expected, but revenue did and so did donations among new visitors. Potential deterrents could be the treatment page layout: The layout includes two images, both of Dr. Carson.
