The images split up the page copy and could really disrupt the flow of value proposition copy. We may want to try using only one image on the page or right aligning the book image in the main body to avoid disrupting the reader's flow.
And perhaps this specific image is not our most effective. We may want try implementing a different image but with the same intention. It may be beneficial to test different variations of images to see which resonates best with potential donors.

experiments.title: How adding additional value proposition language to a main donation page affects donations – attempt #2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding more value proposition language on the main donation page for potential donors will achieve an increase in donations because potential donors will have an improved understanding of the impact their gift would have.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/03/2023 - 03/31/2023
In an effort to increase donation rates on a public policy organization’s main donation page, we wanted to test adding more value prop language to further convey the value of a visitor’s donation.
This test was run only on the “main” donation page, i.e., not a donation page associated with a specific acquisition effort, which is generally reached by clicking on a “make a donation” button on the main navigation bar of the site.
It’s important to note that we ran a previous expanded value proposition test on this main donation page roughly a month ago and it produced a 28% drop in donation conversion rate with a 85.9% level of confidence. The focus of that test was to bring attention to the “reasons why” someone should give. We laid out five reasons via bullet points but the approach was deemed unsuccessful per our audience’s response. You can view the experiment write up at this link: https://app.winstonknows.com/e/pRdg
Our hypothesis stemming from our previous experiment’s results was that that because someone who clicks a “make a donation” button on a website has a high motivation to give, increasing the length and content of the donation page language may create unnecessary friction to the donation process by presenting an overlong wall of copy for them to read. In other words, we suspect that when a potential donor is sufficiently motivated to give, it may be best to “stay out of their way” by using less copy which is more generalized above the donation form.
For this experiment, we eliminated the “reasons why” someone should give – due to the high motivation someone has when clicking “make a donation,” we also hypothesized that they already have at least one reason to give in mind and therefore do not need more reasons presented to them. Here we cut down the copy from our previous test while still expanding the value proposition copy on the current control variant, this time focusing on a broader message of how their gift would help our nation today.
We designed this experiment as an A/B test, with roughly 50% of the traffic sent to the control and roughly 50% of the traffic redirected to the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running for 30 days, this experiment produced flat results. The treatment resulted in a 0.27% decrease in donations with a less than 1% level of confidence. The drop was not as extensive as our previous donation page value proposition experiment, which further supports our hypothesis that the previous experiment's extensive copy was not well-perceived by our potential donors. However, the less extensive, but still enhanced value proposition we used for the treatment in this experiment still did not produce positive results.
Returning visitors saw a 73% increase in donations with an 86.3% level of confidence, while new visitors saw a 34.5% decrease in donations with an 83.3% level of confidence. It's difficult to explain the negative results of new visitors as that's the segment we would expect would benefit from expanded value prop and learning more about our cause.
The key learning from this experiment is that adding more value proposition language to the main donation page may not always be effective in increasing donations. It is important to consider that visitors who click on the "make a donation" button are already highly motivated to give, and adding more copy may create unnecessary friction.
The control variant continues to stand. For a next main donation page test, we may want to consider taking the existing language from our control and expanding specifically on it. The control mentions "America's founding principles" both in the headline and the main copy. Perhaps that phrase has great importance, clarity, and appeal to our audience, and instead of focusing on copy on something else like core pillars, we should use America's founding principles to expand on within our messaging.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to an online education program sign up page impacted email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition language to a program sign up page for page visitors will achieve an increase in emails acquired because page visitors will have a better understanding of the program and the value it provides.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/13/2023 - 03/17/2023
The American Cornerstone Institute has a program called Little Patriots that offers lessons, activities, videos, cartoons, workbooks and more tailored toward children to teach them the story of America. Parents and teachers can sign up on behalf of their children to gain access to this content, and they would do so through the Little Patriots sign up page.
Currently, this page contains just a standard sign up form – it contains no value proposition copy and the headline simply says “REGISTRATION”. We wondered if the implementation of value proposition copy coupled with a descriptive call to action headline would lead to an increase in Little Patriots program sign ups. We’ve run countless tests that support this approach. Audiences typically respond well to value proposition and descriptive call to actions because the elements present value/benefit to the reader/prospect and gives them a clear path to the intended action. Let’s see how the approach works in this scenario…
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment produced a 1.7% lift in email conversion rate with just a 22.6% level of confidence. The results do not dictate that the treatment should be rolled out or applied to the page moving forward. As the vast majority of page visitors come from the Little Patriots home page, there is a good chance they already have a strong understanding of the program and its benefits and do not need the help of value proposition copy on the registration page to convince them to register. In other words, the value is already clear in their minds and registering is just a formality.
The evidence is in the results. The treatment produced a 2.1% decrease in sign ups among returning visitors while new visitors produced a 1.9% lift. Still, the 1.9% lift only had a 22.5% level of confidence.
Paid social traffic produced a 9.6% decrease in email sign ups from the treatment, which could be explained by value being communicated to the user at both the Facebook ad level and the Little Patriots home page before they reach the sign up page.
Ultimately, value proposition may not be the most urgent focus for improving sing up rate. There are other approaches/elements we can try along the lines of social proof or credibility.

experiments.title: How does adding a personalized sticky bar to the donation page affect donation rates during a year-end campaign?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar featuring a public personality to the donation page of a year-end fundraising campaign will increase donation rates by increasing credibility and humanizing the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2022 - 12/28/2022
During a year-end fundraising campaign, we wanted to know how adding a humanized sticky bar to the campaign donation page would affect donation rates for a public policy organization, especially considering that the personalized message on the sticky bar would be coming from a public personality who is well-known and trusted by the organization’s target audience.
For this experiment, we created a treatment featuring a personal message and photograph of a public figure asking users to ‘stand with me’ by making a donation to the year-end campaign. We set this experiment up as an A/B test wherein ~50% of traffic would see the control (no sticky bar) and ~50% of traffic would see the treatment (including the sticky bar). The experiment ran for 23 days, throughout the bulk of the year-end campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 23 days, the treatment featuring a humanized sticky bar achieved a 12.7% increase in donation rates (with a 79% level of confidence) when compared to the control.
We believe that including a sticky bar featuring a well-known public figure who is widely trusted by the target audience was able to increase donations among page visitors by humanizing and building credibility into the appeal. This may also have increased the believability of the outcomes being promoted on the donation page.
And though we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate this increase, we know that there is a relatively low risk associated with implementing a treatment that has shown an increase in targeted metrics, so we decided to roll this treatment out to 100% of traffic to wrap up the campaign.

experiments.title: How a countdown timer on a donation page affects donation rates during the end of a year-end fundraising campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer to the donation page for the last few days of a year-end fundraising campaign will increase donation rates by increasing the urgency for the prospect to respond to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2022 - 01/04/2023
During a year-end fundraising campaign for a public policy organization, we wanted to know how adding a countdown timer to the donation timer during the final four days of the campaign would affect donation rates. We hypothesized that the countdown timer would increase urgency and fear of missing out and encourage more prospects to give before time ran out.
For this brief 4-day experiment, we ran an A/B test with ~50% of traffic seeing the control (no timer) and ~50% of traffic seeing the treatment (countdown timer).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Due to the small sample size entering this experiment, we were unable to draw statistically valid conclusions about the efficacy of using a countdown timer in the context of a high-urgency campaign. Showing the timer produced a 5.4% decrease in conversion rates with only a 38.2% level of confidence.
Although this experiment did not validate, the results suggest that we may not be able to assume that adding a countdown timer to a donation page has a significant impact on donation rates. This could be because it is overdone in the nonprofit fundraising space (thus decreasing its impactfulness), or perhaps because it distracts from the content of the donations page. But due to the low statistical confidence of these results, these are only additional hypotheses and we are unable to draw definitive conclusions one way or the other.
Knowing that there is risk involved with implementing a treatment that produces a decrease in conversions, even if the result is not statistically valid, we will want to test this concept again in an effort to receive an adequate sample size to validate the efficacy of a countdown timer before rolling it out to all traffic during future high-urgency campaigns.

experiments.title: How a tabbed donation form affects recurring giving rates on an acquisition offer
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a tabbed donation form will increase recurring giving rates on the donation page of an acquisition offer by increasing the visibility of the option to give monthly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/27/2022 - 12/02/2022
In an effort to increase the recurring giving rate on an instant donation page of an acquisition offer, we wanted to know how increasing the visibility of the monthly giving option earlier in the donation process, with a tabbed donation form (which in the past has shown success in this regard), would affect recurring monthly giving.
We ran this experiment for 30 days as an A/B test splitting traffic 50/50 between:
A. the control that features a monthly giving checkbox beneath the gift array, and
B. the treatment that removes the checkbox in favor of a monthly giving tab above the gift array
In addition, the impact statement featured below the monthly giving checkbox in the control was moved above the monthly donation form in order to keep it in close proximity to the monthly giving option.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 30 days, the treatment featuring the tabbed donation form produced a 16.6% DECREASE in recurring giving rates with a 47.7% LoC. While the low statistical validity of this experiment prevents us from drawing definitive conclusions about these results one way or another, we did observe that perhaps the checkbox in the control may have offered a more logical "flow" during the donation process, prompting users to first select their donation amount, then select how often they want to give, then view the impact that monthly donation will make.
But because the results of this experiment were well below the 95% LoC that would make them statistically valid, and because we have seen tabbed donation forms produce lifts in recurring giving for other clients in the past, we will want to look for opportunities to test this treatment on a donation page that has an opportunity to produce a larger sample size and validate the results one way to the other.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a donation page affects donations during a Giving Tuesday Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar featuring a well-known public figure during a high-urgency campaign will increase donation rates by adding value and credibility to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/03/2022
During a Giving Tuesday campaign for a public policy client, we wanted to know how adding a humanized sticky bar including the image of a well-known public figure would affect donation rates.
This experiment ran as an A/B test for one day with roughly 50% of traffic being shown the treatment including the sticky bar and roughly 50% of traffic shown the control, sans the sticky bar.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for the length of the Giving Tuesday campaign—one day—we observed a 20.6% DECLINE in donation rates, with a 61% level of confidence, among traffic who were shown the treatment including the humanized sticky bar. Because we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate these results, it is not possible to draw reliable inferences about this experiment.
However, observationally, we can hypothesize that by being large and colorful, the sticky bar may have actually created friction by attracting users' eyes to the top page, rather than the headline and then the body copy, and then the donation form, creating friction during the conversion process.
Because we were unable to obtain a large enough sample size to validate these results, we will want to deploy a similar experiment during a longer-term high-urgency campaign in an effort to validate the results one way or the other.

experiments.title: How does adding a humanized sticky bar to an instant donation page affect instant donation pages?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to an instant donation page will increase instant donation rates for prospects who receive an ebook acquisition offer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/23/2022 - 08/01/2022
In an effort to increase donation rates for an ebook acquisition offer, we wanted to test if adding a humanized sticky bar to the instant donation would connect to prospects and encourage more people to give.
We ran this experiment for 36 days, splitting traffic 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running the experiment for 36 days, we observed a 3.7% lift in instant conversion rates with a 19.8% level of confidence, meaning that there were not enough gifts to validate these results.
It does not appear that in this instance adding a humanized sticky bar inspired significantly more prospective donors to give, however the low number of samples and the resulting low level of confidence in these results prevent us from drawing definitive conclusions one way or the other.
We will look for other opportunities to run this experiment in an effort to validate our hypothesis.

experiments.title: How reducing friction and anxiety by additional secure features impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting gift security to reduce anxiety for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/11/2022
When looking for ways to reduce friction and anxiety on an instant donation page, we wondered if it would be helpful to have additional indications of the security of the gift at the point of conversion. We added a lock icon directly on the button and set up a 50/50 split test to see how donor conversion would be impacted.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running the experiment for about 2 weeks, we were only able to observe a 3.1% increase with 28.4% level of confidence so the test results are not valid. We suspect the results are flat because gift security is already indicated below the button so the additional lock icon might not be necessary. Because we did not see the lock icon cause a decrease in giving, we could try an additional iteration of the test that moves the lock icon positioning to be overlaid on the button for the purpose of replacing the lock icon underneath the donate button and accompanying text with an impact statement highlighting the value of their gift that would motivate them to continue in the giving process.

experiments.title: How framing the visitor as the “solution” impacts survey completions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing the visitor as the key to finding a solution for email prospecting and paid social media audiences will achieve an increase in survey completion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/11/2022 - 01/27/2022
The American Cornerstone Institute was running a survey on the state of education in America. This was being promoted via email prospecting and paid social media. We had a hypothesis that be framing the visitor as the key to finding a solution to the problem would increase their likelihood to take the survey. We decided to test this hypothesis against the existing control version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for two full weeks, we found that there was no statistically significant difference between the control and treatment versions. This would suggest that either the hypothesis is wrong or the treatment did not lean heavily enough into theory to understand the impact.
Interestingly, we did observe an increase of 30% in donor conversion with a 96% level of confidence. However, when digging deeper into the data, we observed that the increase in conversion rate fluctuated greatly from day to day which suggests that there were outside factors impacting the results. We would need to relaunch this experiment to better understand if it truly was impacting donor conversion.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 6.6% increase in email acquisition with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How keeping a countdown clock visible at all times impacted donor converison
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that making the countdown timer visible throughout the process for visitors to the end of year donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2021 - 01/04/2022
At the end of the year, American Cornerstone Institute was running a fundraising campaign to raise money for 2022. Given the December 31 deadline, we incorporated an animated countdown clock at the top of the donation page. After reviewing the page, we questioned whether it would help improve conversion by making the clock always visible on the page. It is possible this could also cause a distraction to the visitor so we decided to test the static banner at the top against one that was sticky and would scroll with the user.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for the final days of the campaign, we did not see any statistically significant difference between the control and treatment. This would imply that the implementation of the countdown clock is less important that simply having it on the page.

experiments.title: How the location of the email capture impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for name and email prior to starting the survey for email subscribers will achieve increased survey completion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/12/2021 - 11/18/2021
American Cornerstone Institute was launching a survey on the state of education in America. This was part of a larger campaign to promote their Little Patriots educational curriculum. The first phase included sending this survey to their email house file.
We had a theory that the position of the name and email capture may influence the likelihood of someone to complete the survey. This is something that is normally reserved to the end of the survey but, we believed that this might actually cause greater frustration and be viewed as a bait and switch. We decided to test this hypothesis with this initial launch.
experiments.keyLearnings: After collecting over 800 submissions, we found that there was no statistically significant difference when asking for the email address prior to starting a survey or at the end. This unlocks the possibility of capturing the email address prior to form submission so that we can send "abandon cart" emails for people who begin the survey but don't finish.
